Arjuna wants to truly understand the basics of reality – what is nature (the physical world), who or what is experiencing this world, what is the world itself, and how do we gain knowledge about it. He's asking Krishna to help him grasp the fundamental difference between the observer and the observed, and the nature of knowledge itself.

**Original Translation:**
Arjuna said: O Keshava, I wish to understand what is Prakriti (nature), Purusha (the enjoyer), and the field and the knower of the field, and what are knowledge and the object of knowledge.

I'm really stressed because my startup is failing. I've poured my heart and soul into it, and I'm afraid of losing everything – my money, my reputation, even my friends who invested with me. I feel completely devastated when things go wrong, and overjoyed when there's a small win, constantly riding an emotional rollercoaster. How can I deal with this emotional upheaval and keep moving forward without being completely broken by the setbacks or overly elated by minor successes?

असक्तिरनभिष्वङ्गः पुत्रदारगृहादिषु |
नित्यं च समचित्तत्वमिष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु ||१३-१०|| (Chapter 13, Verse 10)

**Simple Meaning:**
Don't get overly attached to anything or anyone – family, possessions, or even your goals. Also, learn to stay balanced and calm whether things are going well or badly. It's about finding inner peace regardless of external circumstances.

**Original Translation:**
Non-attachment to children, wife, home, and the rest, and evenmindedness amid desired and undesired events;

I'm feeling overwhelmed by social media and constant notifications. I try to find peace, but I'm always pulled back into checking my phone and comparing myself to others. Everyone seems to have a 'perfect' life online, and I'm losing sight of what *I* actually want. How can I find genuine peace and focus on my own path amidst all this distraction?

मयि चानन्ययोगेन भक्तिरव्यभिचारिणी |
विविक्तदेशसेवित्वमरतिर्जनसंसदि ||१३-११|| (Chapter 13, Verse 11)

**Simple Meaning:**
True devotion means focusing completely on God without distractions, spending time in peaceful solitude to connect with your inner self, and avoiding getting lost in the noise of everyday life and superficial relationships. It's about deeply understanding what truly matters.

**Original Translation:**
Unalloyed devotion unto Me, resorting to solitary places, detachment from the general mass of people, resolute in understanding the object of philosophical knowledge;

I'm constantly bombarded with information – news, social media, opinions from everyone around me. It feels like I'm learning more and more, but I also feel more confused and less sure of what *really* matters in life. How can I find a sense of clarity and focus amidst all this noise, and understand what true 'knowledge' even is in this situation?

अध्यात्मज्ञाननित्यत्वं तत्त्वज्ञानार्थदर्शनम् |
एतज्ज्ञानमिति प्रोक्तमज्ञानं यदतोऽन्यथा ||१३-१२|| (Chapter 13, Verse 12)

**Simple Meaning:**
True knowledge isn't just collecting facts; it's consistently understanding the deeper spiritual truths and having a clear vision of life's ultimate purpose. Anything that distracts you from this consistent understanding and clear purpose is considered ignorance.

**Original Translation:**
Constancy in spiritual knowledge, and perception of the goal of knowledge—this is declared to be knowledge, and what is opposed to this is ignorance.

I'm constantly chasing success and happiness, but nothing ever feels truly satisfying. I get a temporary boost, but then I'm back to feeling empty. I'm starting to wonder if real fulfillment lies *beyond* all these external achievements – something deeper and more lasting. What is that 'something deeper' and how can I find it?

ज्ञेयं यत्तत्प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वामृतमश्नुते |
अनादिमत्परं ब्रह्म न सत्तन्नासदुच्यते ||१३-१३|| (Chapter 13, Verse 13)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna is about to reveal the ultimate truth – knowing this truth will lead to lasting peace and freedom from the cycle of birth and death. This truth is Brahman, a spiritual reality that exists beyond everything we experience in the physical world, and it's connected to the divine source (Krishna). It's always been there and isn't created or destroyed like things in the material world.

**Original Translation:**
I shall now explain the knowable, knowing which you will taste the eternal. Brahman, the spirit, beginningless and subordinate to Me, lies beyond the cause and effect of this material world.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed with anxiety and like I'm disconnected from everything. It feels like there's no safe space or way to truly escape my worries. I've tried meditation, but I still feel lost. If God is truly *everywhere* as this verse says, how can I find peace and connection when I feel so isolated and stressed, and is there a way to feel His presence in my daily struggles?

सर्वतः पाणिपादं तत्सर्वतोऽक्षिशिरोमुखम् |
सर्वतः श्रुतिमल्लोके सर्वमावृत्य तिष्ठति ||१३-१४|| (Chapter 13, Verse 14)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse describes God as being everywhere at once – in everything and all around us. It means God isn't limited to one place or form; He's the very essence of existence, present in every part of the universe and within all living beings. You can experience Him anywhere and everywhere.

**Original Translation:**
Everywhere are His hands and legs, His eyes, heads and faces, and He has ears everywhere. In this way the Supersoul exists, pervading everything.

I'm a doctor constantly dealing with patients' suffering, and it's emotionally draining. I feel responsible for their well-being, but also overwhelmed by the pain and loss I witness daily. How can I continue to provide compassionate care without getting completely consumed by the negativity and remain mentally balanced?

सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासं सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम् |
असक्तं सर्वभृच्चैव निर्गुणं गुणभोक्तृ च ||१३-१५|| (Chapter 13, Verse 15)

**Simple Meaning:**
The Supreme Reality is the source of everything we experience through our senses, but isn't limited by them. It's involved in everything, supporting all life, yet remains detached and unaffected by it all. It's beyond good and bad, but still controls everything that happens.

**Original Translation:**
The Supersoul is the original source of all senses, yet He is without senses. He is unattached, although He is the maintainer of all living beings. He transcends the modes of nature, and at the same time He is the master of all the modes of material nature.

I'm going through a really tough time, feeling lost and disconnected from everything. I try to find meaning in life and feel a connection to something bigger, but it just feels impossible. It's like there's a vast emptiness all around me. How can I feel closer to something real and find peace when everything feels so far away and unknowable?

बहिरन्तश्च भूतानामचरं चरमेव च |
सूक्ष्मत्वात्तदविज्ञेयं दूरस्थं चान्तिके च तत् ||१३-१६|| (Chapter 13, Verse 16)

**Simple Meaning:**
God or the ultimate reality is everywhere – within everything that lives and exists, and also beyond our normal understanding. It’s hard to perceive with our senses because it's incredibly subtle, but even though it feels distant, it’s actually always right here with us.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Truth exists outside and inside of all living beings, the moving and the nonmoving. Because He is subtle, He is beyond the power of the material senses to see or to know. Although far, far away, He is also near to all.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed by the world – it feels like so much suffering and loss is happening everywhere, and I’m struggling to understand how a loving God can allow it. It feels like everything is separate and random, but deep down, I feel like there has to be a bigger picture. How can I reconcile the presence of pain and chaos with the idea of a single, unifying divine force?

अविभक्तं च भूतेषु विभक्तमिव च स्थितम् |
भूतभर्तृ च तज्ज्ञेयं ग्रसिष्णु प्रभविष्णु च ||१३-१७|| (Chapter 13, Verse 17)

**Simple Meaning:**
The divine presence is in everything and everyone, like a single energy spread throughout the universe. It appears separate, but it's actually one unified power. This power sustains everything, bringing it into being and eventually taking it back – it’s a continuous cycle of creation and dissolution.

**Original Translation:**
Although the Supersoul appears to be divided among all beings, He is never divided. He is situated as one. Although He is the maintainer of every living entity, it is to be understood that He devours and develops all.

I'm feeling really lost and confused about my life's purpose. I've tried so many things – different careers, hobbies, even philosophies – but nothing seems to fit. It feels like I'm searching for something outside of myself, but I can't find it. Where do I even begin to find meaning when everything feels empty, and is it possible the answer isn't 'out there' somewhere?

ज्योतिषामपि तज्ज्योतिस्तमसः परमुच्यते |
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं ज्ञानगम्यं हृदि सर्वस्य विष्ठितम् ||१३-१८|| (Chapter 13, Verse 18)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that the Divine is the light within everything bright, but also exists beyond what we can see or understand. It’s the true knowledge itself, what we try to know, and the ultimate goal of learning – and it’s already inside each of us. Basically, the divine is the source, the knowledge, and resides within everyone.

**Original Translation:**
He is the source of light in all luminous objects. He is beyond the darkness of matter and is unmanifested. He is knowledge, He is the object of knowledge, and He is the goal of knowledge. He is situated in everyone’s heart.

I'm feeling really lost in life, like I'm chasing goals but nothing feels meaningful. I've tried self-help books and therapy, but it hasn't clicked. I intellectually understand concepts like purpose and connection, but it doesn't resonate with me on a deeper level. What's missing, and how can I find that true understanding?

इति क्षेत्रं तथा ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं चोक्तं समासतः |
मद्भक्त एतद्विज्ञाय मद्भावायोपपद्यते ||१३-१९|| (Chapter 13, Verse 19)

**Simple Meaning:**
I've explained everything – life, knowledge, and what there is to know. But only those who are truly devoted to me can *really* grasp it and connect with the deeper truth of existence, ultimately realizing their connection to the divine.

**Original Translation:**
Thus the field of activities [the body], knowledge and the knowable have been summarily described by Me. Only My devotees can understand this thoroughly and thus attain to My nature.

I've been obsessing over my appearance – worrying about wrinkles, weight gain, and how others see me. It's causing me so much stress and anxiety. How can I stop letting my physical body define my self-worth and find peace, especially when I feel so insecure about changes to it?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते |
एतद्यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञ इति तद्विदः ||१३-२|| (Chapter 13, Verse 2)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna explains to Arjuna that your physical body isn't 'you' – it's more like a vessel or a field. The real 'you' is the one who is *aware* of this body. Knowing this distinction – separating the self from the body – is true understanding.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O son of Kunti, this body is called the field, and one who knows this body is called the knower of the field.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed by all the ups and downs in my life – my moods change constantly, and things never seem stable. Sometimes I’m happy, sometimes sad, sometimes anxious. It feels like I have no control over my feelings or the events happening to me. How can I find peace when everything feels so temporary and constantly shifting?

प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव विद्ध्यनादी उभावपि |
विकारांश्च गुणांश्चैव विद्धि प्रकृतिसम्भवान् ||१३-२०|| (Chapter 13, Verse 20)

**Simple Meaning:**
Both the physical world around us (nature) and the individual souls within it have always existed. Everything we see changing – emotions, experiences, qualities – comes from nature itself.  It’s a cycle of creation and change originating from the basic elements of existence.

**Original Translation:**
Material nature and the living entities should be understood to be beginningless. Their transformations and the modes of matter are products of material nature.

I'm working really hard at my new business, following all the right strategies, but I'm still facing constant setbacks and stress. It feels unfair and exhausting. Sometimes I feel like it's all just bad luck, and other times I blame myself completely. How do I find balance in understanding what’s in my control and what isn't, so I can keep going without losing my mind?

कार्यकारणकर्तृत्वे हेतुः प्रकृतिरुच्यते |
पुरुषः सुखदुःखानां भोक्तृत्वे हेतुरुच्यते ||१३-२१|| (Chapter 13, Verse 21)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse explains that nature (like the physical world and its laws) causes things to happen – the 'how' of events. But *we*, as individuals, experience the results – the good and the bad feelings that come from those events. Basically, nature sets things in motion, and we feel the consequences.

**Original Translation:**
Nature is said to be the cause of all material causes and effects, whereas the living entity is the cause of the various sufferings and enjoyments in this world.

I'm constantly feeling stressed and unhappy at my new job, even though it's a good opportunity. It feels like no matter what I do, negativity follows me. My friend keeps telling me I need a more positive outlook, but it feels deeper than that. Why do I keep experiencing this negativity, and is it possible to change my circumstances, or do I just need to accept this as my fate?

पुरुषः प्रकृतिस्थो हि भुङ्क्ते प्रकृतिजान्गुणान् |
कारणं गुणसङ्गोऽस्य सदसद्योनिजन्मसु ||१३-२२|| (Chapter 13, Verse 22)

**Simple Meaning:**
We experience life – both good and bad – because of the qualities of nature around us and the way we connect with them. Our experiences, whether positive or negative, are shaped by this connection and influence our rebirths into different forms of life.

**Original Translation:**
The living entity in material nature thus follows the ways of life, enjoying the three modes of nature. This is due to his association with that material nature. Thus he meets with good and evil among various species.

I'm going through a really tough time with my career and relationships, and I feel completely lost and out of control. I keep questioning what the point of it all is. How can I find peace and understand my role in everything when it feels like life is just happening *to* me, and I don’t have any say in it?

उपद्रष्टानुमन्ता च भर्ता भोक्ता महेश्वरः |
परमात्मेति चाप्युक्तो देहेऽस्मिन्पुरुषः परः ||१३-२३|| (Chapter 13, Verse 23)

**Simple Meaning:**
There's a higher self within each of us – a part of God – that's always watching over everything and allowing things to happen. It's the ultimate owner and enjoyer of life's experiences, but it doesn't interfere directly. It’s like a silent witness within your own body.

**Original Translation:**
Yet in this body there is another, a transcendental enjoyer, who is the Lord, the supreme proprietor, who exists as the overseer and permitter, and who is known as the Supersoul.

I'm constantly stressed and feel trapped in a cycle of trying to achieve more, but nothing ever truly satisfies me. Even when I get what I want, I quickly feel empty again. I understand I'm more than just my job and possessions, but I still feel stuck. How can I break free from this endless loop of desire and dissatisfaction, and find lasting peace, even while still navigating the world?

य एवं वेत्ति पुरुषं प्रकृतिं च गुणैः सह |
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि न स भूयोऽभिजायते ||१३-२४|| (Chapter 13, Verse 24)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you truly understand how the world works – that you're not the material body, and how energies (like good, bad, and neutral influences) affect everything – you'll be freed from the cycle of birth and rebirth, no matter what situation you're in right now.

**Original Translation:**
One who understands this philosophy concerning material nature, the living entity and the interaction of the modes of nature is sure to attain liberation. He will not take birth here again, regardless of his present position.

I'm feeling really lost about my purpose. My friend keeps telling me I need to find time for daily meditation, but my job is really demanding, and honestly, I find peace in *doing* things, in being productive. Is it okay if I focus on doing my best work and contributing to society, even if I don't have a traditional spiritual practice? Is one path 'better' than another?

ध्यानेनात्मनि पश्यन्ति केचिदात्मानमात्मना |
अन्ये साङ्ख्येन योगेन कर्मयोगेन चापरे ||१३-२५|| (Chapter 13, Verse 25)

**Simple Meaning:**
People find the divine truth in different ways. Some connect through deep thinking and meditation, others through study and understanding, and yet others by dedicating themselves to their work without worrying about the results. There isn't just one right path.

**Original Translation:**
Some perceive the Supersoul within themselves through meditation, others through the cultivation of knowledge, and still others through working without fruitive desires.

My grandmother always talks about her faith and tells me stories about God, even though I'm very skeptical and haven't really explored religion myself. Could simply listening to her and her beliefs still be a meaningful path towards peace and a better life, even if I don't fully understand or agree with everything she says?

अन्ये त्वेवमजानन्तः श्रुत्वान्येभ्य उपासते |
तेऽपि चातितरन्त्येव मृत्युं श्रुतिपरायणाः ||१३-२६|| (Chapter 13, Verse 26)

**Simple Meaning:**
Some people haven't studied spirituality deeply, but they still make progress by simply listening to stories and teachings about God from others. Just hearing about the Divine with faith can help them overcome life and death cycles.

**Original Translation:**
Again there are those who, although not conversant in spiritual knowledge, begin to worship the Supreme Person upon hearing about Him from others. Because of their tendency to hear from authorities, they also transcend the path of birth and death.

I’m really stressed about losing my job. It feels like my whole identity is wrapped up in what I do, and I'm worried I'll lose everything if I lose this role. How can I remember that *I* am more than just my job or what I achieve? How can I feel less attached to the outcome and understand that my core self isn't dependent on external things?

यावत्सञ्जायते किञ्चित्सत्त्वं स्थावरजङ्गमम् |
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञसंयोगात्तद्विद्धि भरतर्षभ ||१३-२७|| (Chapter 13, Verse 27)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything you see around you – living things, objects, everything – is a mix of energy and the consciousness experiencing that energy. It's like a combination of 'what happens' and 'who observes it happening'. Nothing exists independently of this combination.

**Original Translation:**
O chief of the Bhāratas, know that whatever you see in existence, both the moving and the nonmoving, is only a combination of the field of activities and the knower of the field.

My grandmother passed away recently, and I'm really struggling with grief. It feels like a part of her is just…gone. People keep telling me to move on, but it's hard. How can I reconcile my sadness with the idea that something of her still exists, and how can understanding that help me cope with this loss?

समं सर्वेषु भूतेषु तिष्ठन्तं परमेश्वरम् |
विनश्यत्स्वविनश्यन्तं यः पश्यति स पश्यति ||१३-२८|| (Chapter 13, Verse 28)

**Simple Meaning:**
The verse says that true vision comes from realizing that God (the Supersoul) is present within *everyone* and everything. And importantly, understanding that both the individual soul and God are eternal – they don't die with the body. When you see this truth, you *really* see.

**Original Translation:**
One who sees the Supersoul accompanying the individual soul in all bodies, and who understands that neither the soul nor the Supersoul within the destructible body is ever destroyed, actually sees.

I'm constantly getting into arguments with my family and colleagues because I feel they're 'wrong' and I'm 'right'. It's exhausting and damaging relationships. How can I find peace and stop feeling the need to constantly correct others and see things my way?

समं पश्यन्हि सर्वत्र समवस्थितमीश्वरम् |
न हिनस्त्यात्मनात्मानं ततो याति परां गतिम् ||१३-२९|| (Chapter 13, Verse 29)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you recognize the same divine spirit within everyone and everything, you won't judge or harm others, and that's how you move closer to spiritual fulfillment.

**Original Translation:**
One who sees the Supersoul equally present everywhere, in every living being, does not degrade himself by his mind. Thus he approaches the transcendental destination.

I'm really struggling with feeling disconnected from others, like everyone is so different from me. I get caught up in judging people based on their appearance or what they do, and it makes it hard to build genuine relationships. How can I remember that we're all fundamentally connected and stop feeling so separate?

क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि सर्वक्षेत्रेषु भारत |
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोर्ज्ञानं यत्तज्ज्ञानं मतं मम ||१३-३|| (Chapter 13, Verse 3)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, understand that the true self, the consciousness within each being (including you and me), is the same everywhere. Real wisdom comes from recognizing this connection – knowing both the body and the soul inhabiting it. That's my understanding of true knowledge.

**Original Translation:**
O scion of Bharata, you should understand that I am also the knower in all bodies, and to understand this body and its knower is called knowledge. That is My opinion.

I'm a really competitive entrepreneur, and I constantly stress about my business's success or failure. I feel responsible for everything – every deal, every setback. It's exhausting! How can I deal with this constant pressure and not feel like my entire self-worth is tied to my company's performance?

प्रकृत्यैव च कर्माणि क्रियमाणानि सर्वशः |
यः पश्यति तथात्मानमकर्तारं स पश्यति ||१३-३०|| (Chapter 13, Verse 30)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that when you realize all actions are happening *through* your body and mind – which are just tools made of nature – and that *you*, your true self, are simply the witness, you start to understand things as they really are. It's about detaching from the idea that 'I am doing' and seeing yourself as the observer, not the doer.

**Original Translation:**
One who can see that all activities are performed by the body, which is created of material nature, and sees that the self does nothing, actually sees.

I'm constantly stressed about comparing myself to others on social media. It feels like everyone is living a better life, and I'm stuck in a cycle of feeling inadequate. How can I stop focusing on these differences and find peace with who I am?

यदा भूतपृथग्भावमेकस्थमनुपश्यति |
तत एव च विस्तारं ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते तदा ||१३-३१|| (Chapter 13, Verse 31)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you stop focusing on what makes people different – like their background, appearance, or possessions – and instead recognize the underlying unity of all beings, you start to understand the true nature of reality, which is Brahman – a universal, interconnected consciousness.

**Original Translation:**
When a sensible man ceases to see different identities due to different material bodies and he sees how beings are expanded everywhere, he attains to the Brahman conception.

I'm going through a really tough time at work. I made some decisions I regret and feel deeply guilty and ashamed. It's affecting my self-worth and I can't seem to move on. How can I separate my mistakes from who I truly am, and find peace knowing I'm more than just my actions?

अनादित्वान्निर्गुणत्वात्परमात्मायमव्ययः |
शरीरस्थोऽपि कौन्तेय न करोति न लिप्यते ||१३-३२|| (Chapter 13, Verse 32)

**Simple Meaning:**
The true self, your soul, is unchanging and always exists. It's separate from your body and the changing world around you. Even though it's *in* the body, it doesn't get involved in actions or get stained by their consequences. It's a pure observer.

**Original Translation:**
Those with the vision of eternity can see that the imperishable soul is transcendental, eternal, and beyond the modes of nature. Despite contact with the material body, O Arjuna, the soul neither does anything nor is entangled.

I'm going through a really tough time – lots of stress at work, family issues, and generally feeling overwhelmed. It feels like all these problems are *me*, like they define who I am. How can I remember that there's a part of me that is separate from all this chaos and doesn't get ruined by it?

यथा सर्वगतं सौक्ष्म्यादाकाशं नोपलिप्यते |
सर्वत्रावस्थितो देहे तथात्मा नोपलिप्यते ||१३-३३|| (Chapter 13, Verse 33)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like the sky is everywhere but stays pure and doesn't get dirty or attached to anything, your true self (Atma) is present in your body but isn't affected by its experiences or limitations. It's separate and untouched by the physical world.

**Original Translation:**
The sky, due to its subtle nature, does not mix with anything, although it is all-pervading. Similarly, the soul situated in Brahman vision does not mix with the body, though situated in that body.

I've been feeling really down lately, like I'm just going through the motions and disconnected from everything. I focus so much on my physical health and achievements, but still feel empty. How can I find a sense of purpose and connection to something deeper than just my body and daily routine?

यथा प्रकाशयत्येकः कृत्स्नं लोकमिमं रविः |
क्षेत्रं क्षेत्री तथा कृत्स्नं प्रकाशयति भारत ||१३-३४|| (Chapter 13, Verse 34)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like the sun lights up the whole world, your true self – your consciousness – lights up your entire body and mind. You aren't just the body; there's an inner light making you aware and alive.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Bharata, as the sun alone illuminates all this universe, so does the living entity, one within the body, illuminate the entire body by consciousness.

I'm constantly chasing the next promotion, bigger house, and more possessions, but I never feel satisfied. I'm always worried about losing what I have, and it feels like I'm trapped in a cycle of wanting more. How can I find lasting peace and contentment instead of being driven by these external things?

क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोरेवमन्तरं ज्ञानचक्षुषा |
भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षं च ये विदुर्यान्ति ते परम् ||१३-३५|| (Chapter 13, Verse 35)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you can understand that you are not your body, but a separate consciousness within it, and also figure out how to break free from being controlled by your desires and worldly attachments, you'll reach true freedom and happiness.

**Original Translation:**
Those who see with eyes of knowledge the difference between the body and the knower of the body, and can also understand the process of liberation from bondage in material nature, attain to the supreme goal.

I'm constantly worried about my health, my job, and my future. Everything feels so temporary and unstable. I feel like I *am* my body and my circumstances. How can I find lasting peace and understand who I really am amidst all this change and uncertainty?

ॐ तत्सदिति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु
ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञविभागयोगो नाम त्रयोदशोऽध्यायः ||१३|| (Chapter 13, Verse 36)

**Simple Meaning:**
This is the introduction to the thirteenth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, called 'The Yoga of the Distinction Between the Field and the Knower of the Field'. It sets the stage for understanding the difference between the body (the 'field') and the true self, the eternal soul or consciousness (the 'knower of the field'). It's about realizing what you truly *are* beyond your physical existence.

**Original Translation:**
Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

I'm feeling totally lost and overwhelmed by everything happening in my life – job stress, family issues, and just the general chaos of the world. It feels like things are constantly changing and I don't know what's real or what matters anymore. How do I get a better understanding of what's *really* going on and find some peace amidst it all?

तत्क्षेत्रं यच्च यादृक्च यद्विकारि यतश्च यत् |
स च यो यत्प्रभावश्च तत्समासेन मे शृणु ||१३-४|| (Chapter 13, Verse 4)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna is about to explain everything about life – the world around us, how things change, where it all comes from, who truly understands it, and how that understanding affects everything. He's giving a complete overview of existence.

**Original Translation:**
Now please hear My brief description of this field of activity and how it is constituted, what its changes are, whence it is produced, who that knower of the field of activities is, and what his influences are.

I'm overwhelmed by all the different self-help books, philosophies, and life advice out there. Everyone seems to have a different opinion on what makes a good life. How do I figure out what's actually true and valuable, and not just get lost in conflicting ideas?

ऋषिभिर्बहुधा गीतं छन्दोभिर्विविधैः पृथक् |
ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैश्चैव हेतुमद्भिर्विनिश्चितैः ||१३-५|| (Chapter 13, Verse 5)

**Simple Meaning:**
This wisdom about life, our purpose, and how things work isn't new. It's been discussed by many wise people throughout history in different scriptures and philosophical texts like the Vedanta-sutra, which explains it logically and with clear reasons.

**Original Translation:**
That knowledge of the field of activities and of the knower of activities is described by various sages in various Vedic writings. It is especially presented in Vedānta-sūtra with all reasoning as to cause and effect.

I'm feeling completely overwhelmed by everything going on in my life – work stress, family issues, my own anxieties, and constantly chasing what I *think* will make me happy. It feels like a never-ending cycle. How can I find some peace when it feels like my own mind and emotions are constantly throwing things at me?

महाभूतान्यहंकारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च |
इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः ||१३-६|| (Chapter 13, Verse 6)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything you experience – your body, thoughts, feelings, senses, desires, and even your beliefs – makes up the world you interact with. This verse reminds us that all of this is part of a bigger system, a field of action where things are constantly happening and changing.

**Original Translation:**
The great elements, false ego, intelligence, the unmanifested, the ten senses, the mind, the five sense objects, desire, hatred, happiness, distress, the aggregate, the life symptoms, and convictions—all these are considered, in summary, to be the field of activities and its interactions.

I'm constantly stressed trying to control my life – I want good things to happen and really dislike when things go wrong. I chase happiness and avoid sadness. It feels like a never-ending battle. How can I find peace when life is just this constant rollercoaster of wanting and not wanting, joy and sorrow? Is there a way to step back and deal with it all without being so affected?

इच्छा द्वेषः सुखं दुःखं संघातश्चेतना धृतिः |
एतत्क्षेत्रं समासेन सविकारमुदाहृतम् ||१३-७|| (Chapter 13, Verse 7)

**Simple Meaning:**
Basically, everything you experience – wanting things, disliking things, feeling happy or sad, your physical body and your beliefs – all of this makes up the 'field' where life happens. It’s the complete package of your existence, constantly changing and interacting.

**Original Translation:**
Desire and hatred, happiness and distress, the aggregate, the life symptoms, and convictions—all these are considered, in summary, to be the field of activities and its interactions.

I'm constantly comparing myself to others on social media and feel really insecure about my progress. I often get into arguments online and feel like I’m losing my patience and focus. How can I cultivate a more peaceful mindset and stop getting caught up in negativity and competition to focus on my own growth?

अमानित्वमदम्भित्वमहिंसा क्षान्तिरार्जवम् |
आचार्योपासनं शौचं स्थैर्यमात्मविनिग्रहः ||१३-८|| (Chapter 13, Verse 8)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse lists qualities of someone who's growing spiritually. It's about being humble, not arrogant, peaceful, honest, and disciplined. It also emphasizes learning from a good teacher and keeping yourself clean – both physically and mentally. Basically, it's about inner strength and peaceful character.

**Original Translation:**
Humility, pridelessness, nonviolence, tolerance, simplicity, approaching a bona fide spiritual master, cleanliness, steadiness and self-control;

I'm constantly stressed about my career, getting older, and worrying about my health. I feel like I need more success, a better body, and to avoid getting sick.  This constant chasing is exhausting me. How can I find some peace and stop feeling so anxious about these things?

इन्द्रियार्थेषु वैराग्यमनहंकार एव च |
जन्ममृत्युजराव्याधिदुःखदोषानुदर्शनम् ||१३-९|| (Chapter 13, Verse 9)

**Simple Meaning:**
True happiness isn't found in chasing pleasures, believing in your own importance, or fearing life's difficulties like birth, death, aging and sickness. Letting go of these attachments and recognizing their inevitable downsides helps you find peace.

**Original Translation:**
Detachment from the objects of the senses, absence of false ego, the perception of the evil of birth, death, old age, and disease;

I feel lost and unfulfilled despite achieving career success and having a comfortable life. I've tried everything – new hobbies, relationships, travel – but nothing seems to bring lasting happiness. Is there a deeper understanding or knowledge I'm missing that could actually help me find true contentment and purpose?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
परं भूयः प्रवक्ष्यामि ज्ञानानां ज्ञानमुत्तमम् |
यज्ज्ञात्वा मुनयः सर्वे परां सिद्धिमितो गताः ||१४-१|| (Chapter 14, Verse 1)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna is about to share the most important knowledge – the kind that, once understood, leads to complete fulfillment and liberation. It’s the wisdom that all great sages have used to reach their highest potential.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: Again I shall declare to you this supreme wisdom, the best of all knowledge, knowing which all the sages have attained the supreme perfection.

I've been really productive and focused at work lately, crushing my goals. But now I'm completely burnt out, feeling unmotivated and exhausted. One week I feel like I can conquer the world, the next I can barely get out of bed. Why is my energy and focus so inconsistent, and how do I deal with these ups and downs?

रजस्तमश्चाभिभूय सत्त्वं भवति भारत |
रजः सत्त्वं तमश्चैव तमः सत्त्वं रजस्तथा ||१४-१०|| (Chapter 14, Verse 10)

**Simple Meaning:**
Life isn't always stable. Sometimes you feel good and positive, sometimes you're driven by desires and ambition, and sometimes you feel dull or negative. These feelings aren't permanent – they come and go as one dominates the other. It’s a constant push and pull.

**Original Translation:**
Sometimes the mode of goodness becomes prominent, defeating the modes of passion and ignorance, O son of Bharata. Sometimes the mode of passion defeats goodness and ignorance, and at other times ignorance defeats goodness and passion. In this way there is always competition for supremacy.

I've been feeling really overwhelmed and confused lately, constantly stressed about work and personal life. It's hard to focus, and I feel anxious all the time. Is there a way to find inner peace and clear thinking amidst all this chaos, so I can make better decisions and feel more grounded?

सर्वद्वारेषु देहेऽस्मिन्प्रकाश उपजायते |
ज्ञानं यदा तदा विद्याद्विवृद्धं सत्त्वमित्युत ||१४-११|| (Chapter 14, Verse 11)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you feel a clear understanding and wisdom shining through all aspects of your being – your senses, mind, and body – that's a sign that the quality of goodness (sattva) is increasing within you. It's like inner clarity and peace spreading throughout your whole self.

**Original Translation:**
The manifestation of the mode of goodness can be experienced when all the gates of the body are illuminated by knowledge.

I'm a startup founder, and I'm obsessed with making my company successful. I'm working 80-hour weeks, constantly stressed about funding and competition, and I feel terrible if we have even a small setback. It's hard to enjoy anything outside of work. Is this level of drive healthy, or am I getting carried away by my ambition?

लोभः प्रवृत्तिरारम्भः कर्मणामशमः स्पृहा |
रजस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे भरतर्षभ ||१४-१२|| (Chapter 14, Verse 12)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you're really driven by wanting things and achieving goals – when ambition and desire take over – you'll find yourself getting overly attached to the results, constantly striving, and feeling restless if you don't get what you want. These feelings are all signs of being caught up in the 'mode of passion'.

**Original Translation:**
O chief of the Bhāratas, when there is an increase in the mode of passion the symptoms of great attachment, fruitive activity, intense endeavor, and uncontrollable desire and hankering develop.

I've been feeling super overwhelmed with stress at work and at home lately. I'm constantly tired, I'm making really impulsive decisions I regret, and I'm starting to feel like I'm not even thinking clearly anymore. It's like I'm just going through the motions. How can I get out of this cycle and start feeling grounded and focused again?

अप्रकाशोऽप्रवृत्तिश्च प्रमादो मोह एव च |
तमस्येतानि जायन्ते विवृद्धे कुरुनन्दन ||१४-१३|| (Chapter 14, Verse 13)

**Simple Meaning:**
When we let negativity and confusion take over our minds, things get unclear, we lose motivation, we act irrationally, and we get lost in delusions. Basically, when we’re overwhelmed by darkness inside, we make bad choices and feel disconnected from reality.

**Original Translation:**
When there is an increase in the mode of ignorance, O son of Kuru, darkness, inertia, madness and illusion are manifested.

My grandmother recently passed away. She was a really good person, always volunteering and helping others, and she was very spiritually inclined. I'm feeling a lot of grief, but also a sense of hope. The verse talks about 'higher planets' - what does that even *mean* in a modern context, and how can understanding this help me cope with her loss and feel at peace knowing where she might be now?

यदा सत्त्वे प्रवृद्धे तु प्रलयं याति देहभृत् |
तदोत्तमविदां लोकानमलान्प्रतिपद्यते ||१४-१४|| (Chapter 14, Verse 14)

**Simple Meaning:**
When someone passes away while living a life focused on goodness – being kind, honest, and knowledgeable – they move on to a higher, more peaceful existence filled with wisdom and purity. It's like graduating to a better place based on how you lived.

**Original Translation:**
When one dies in the mode of goodness, he attains to the pure higher planets of the great sages.

My friend, Priya, is completely obsessed with her career. She works 80-hour weeks, constantly stressing about promotions and recognition, and she can't enjoy anything without thinking about how it benefits her work. She's completely burnt out, but feels she can’t stop.  She keeps saying she’s afraid of losing her status and success. Knowing that things change, and recognizing her intense attachment to results, what advice, based on this verse, could help her understand what future she may be creating for herself if she doesn't change her mindset?

रजसि प्रलयं गत्वा कर्मसङ्गिषु जायते |
तथा प्रलीनस्तमसि मूढयोनिषु जायते ||१४-१५|| (Chapter 14, Verse 15)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you die while focused on getting rewards for your actions (passion), you'll likely be reborn into a life of constantly chasing goals and desires. If you die lost in delusion and ignorance, you might be reborn into a simpler existence, like an animal, where basic instincts rule. Basically, your state of mind at death shapes your next life.

**Original Translation:**
When one dies in the mode of passion, he takes birth among those engaged in fruitive activities; and when one dies in the mode of ignorance, he takes birth in the animal kingdom.

I've been working really hard on a new project at work, but I'm doing it mostly to impress my boss and get a promotion. I feel stressed and anxious all the time, and even if I succeed, I don't feel satisfied. I'm starting to wonder if chasing this goal is actually making me *less* happy. Is there a better way to approach my work so I can feel more peaceful and fulfilled?

कर्मणः सुकृतस्याहुः सात्त्विकं निर्मलं फलम् |
रजसस्तु फलं दुःखमज्ञानं तमसः फलम् ||१४-१६|| (Chapter 14, Verse 16)

**Simple Meaning:**
Good deeds lead to happiness and clarity. Actions driven by desire or ego cause suffering and confusion. And actions done without thought or awareness just lead to more problems and keep you stuck. Basically, the *quality* of your actions determines the results you get.

**Original Translation:**
The result of pious action is pure and is said to be in the mode of goodness. But action done in the mode of passion results in misery, and action performed in the mode of ignorance results in foolishness.

I'm constantly torn between studying for my exams (which feels good but takes effort) and scrolling through social media (which feels good *right now* but I know isn't productive). I feel like I'm constantly switching back and forth, and I'm getting less and less done. Why am I struggling with this, and how can I find more focus and make better choices?

सत्त्वात्सञ्जायते ज्ञानं रजसो लोभ एव च |
प्रमादमोहौ तमसो भवतोऽज्ञानमेव च ||१४-१७|| (Chapter 14, Verse 17)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse explains that where your mind is focused determines the qualities that grow within you. When you're calm and good, knowledge increases. When you're driven by desire and wanting, greed takes over. And when you're confused or lazy, foolishness and illusions cloud your judgment.

**Original Translation:**
From the mode of goodness, real knowledge develops; from the mode of passion, greed develops; and from the mode of ignorance develop foolishness, madness and illusion.

I'm constantly stuck in a cycle of drama with my friends – always fighting, gossiping, and feeling anxious. I know it’s not healthy, but I keep getting pulled back in.  I feel like my life is going nowhere. How can I break free from this negativity and start moving towards a more peaceful and fulfilling life, considering this pattern of behavior is holding me back?

ऊर्ध्वं गच्छन्ति सत्त्वस्था मध्ये तिष्ठन्ति राजसाः |
जघन्यगुणवृत्तिस्था अधो गच्छन्ति तामसाः ||१४-१८|| (Chapter 14, Verse 18)

**Simple Meaning:**
Our actions and mindset determine our future. Good deeds and a positive attitude lead to growth and better experiences, while selfish actions and negative thinking hold us back or lead to suffering. Basically, what you put out comes back to you – a positive outlook leads to a brighter future, and a negative one leads to a darker one.

**Original Translation:**
Those situated in the mode of goodness gradually go upward to the higher planets; those in the mode of passion live on the earthly planets; and those in the abominable mode of ignorance go down to the hellish worlds.

I'm constantly stressed about achieving my career goals. I feel like my happiness depends on my performance, and I'm crushed when things don't go my way. How can I stop feeling so personally responsible for everything and find some inner calm, even when I face setbacks?

नान्यं गुणेभ्यः कर्तारं यदा द्रष्टानुपश्यति |
गुणेभ्यश्च परं वेत्ति मद्भावं सोऽधिगच्छति ||१४-१९|| (Chapter 14, Verse 19)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that everything happening around you – success, failure, joy, sorrow – is actually just the play of nature's forces. When you realize this and understand that a higher power, beyond these forces, is the true doer, you can connect with a deeper, spiritual reality and find peace.

**Original Translation:**
When one properly sees that in all activities no other performer is at work than these modes of nature and he knows the Supreme Lord, who is transcendental to all these modes, he attains My spiritual nature.

I'm constantly stressed about building a successful career and leaving a legacy, but I'm also terrified of failure and the idea that everything I work for will eventually disappear. How can I find peace and purpose without being so attached to outcomes and the fear of everything being temporary?

इदं ज्ञानमुपाश्रित्य मम साधर्म्यमागताः |
सर्गेऽपि नोपजायन्ते प्रलये न व्यथन्ति च ||१४-२|| (Chapter 14, Verse 2)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you truly understand and live by this wisdom, you connect with the divine reality, just like Krishna does. This understanding frees you from the cycle of birth and death, and you won't be troubled by life's ups and downs or the fear of endings.

**Original Translation:**
By becoming fixed in this knowledge, one can attain to the transcendental nature like My own. Thus established, one is not born at the time of creation or disturbed at the time of dissolution.

I'm constantly stressed about work, then stressed about relaxing because I feel guilty not working, and then stressed about my health because of all the stress! It feels like a never-ending loop of worrying about everything. How can I break free from this constant cycle of feeling unhappy and find some real peace, even while still dealing with my everyday life?

गुणानेतानतीत्य त्रीन्देही देहसमुद्भवान् |
जन्ममृत्युजरादुःखैर्विमुक्तोऽमृतमश्नुते ||१४-२०|| (Chapter 14, Verse 20)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that when you rise above the good, bad, and neutral qualities that affect your body and mind, you free yourself from the cycle of suffering – being born, dying, and experiencing pain.  You can find peace and happiness *right now*, not just after death.

**Original Translation:**
When the embodied being is able to transcend these three modes associated with the material body, he can become free from birth, death, old age and their distresses and can enjoy nectar even in this life.

I'm constantly stressed about work, family, and everything in between. I feel like my mood swings wildly depending on what's happening around me. Is it possible to find a place of inner peace where things don’t bother me so easily, and if so, how do I even begin to work towards that?

अर्जुन उवाच |
कैर्लिङ्गैस्त्रीन्गुणानेतानतीतो भवति प्रभो |
किमाचारः कथं चैतांस्त्रीन्गुणानतिवर्तते ||१४-२१|| (Chapter 14, Verse 21)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna is asking Krishna how to recognize someone who's beyond the influence of good, bad, and neutral feelings (the three modes of nature). He wants to know what qualities these people have and how they manage to stay balanced and unaffected by the ups and downs of life.

**Original Translation:**
Arjuna inquired: O my dear Lord, by which symptoms is one known who is transcendental to these three modes? What is his behavior? And how does he transcend the modes of nature?

I'm a freelancer and constantly stressed about getting good projects versus bad ones. I feel like my happiness depends on the kind of work I get. How can I find peace and not let my work, whether I enjoy it or not, control my emotional state?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
प्रकाशं च प्रवृत्तिं च मोहमेव च पाण्डव |
न द्वेष्टि सम्प्रवृत्तानि न निवृत्तानि काङ्क्षति ||१४-२२|| (Chapter 14, Verse 22)

**Simple Meaning:**
God doesn't get caught up in good or bad experiences. Everything – joy, sorrow, activity, or even confusion – happens around Him, but He remains unaffected and doesn't crave or reject any of it. He's a neutral observer.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O son of Pandu, the transcendental qualities such as light, activity, and delusion are present all around, but they are not hated or desired by Me.

I'm constantly stressed about work and my family's problems. I feel like my mood is always changing depending on what’s happening around me.  How can I stop letting external situations control my inner peace and stay calm, even when life gets chaotic?

उदासीनवदासीनो गुणैर्यो न विचाल्यते |
गुणा वर्तन्त इत्येवं योऽवतिष्ठति नेङ्गते ||१४-२३|| (Chapter 14, Verse 23)

**Simple Meaning:**
Like the sky remains clear no matter what winds carry through it, your true self is separate from your thoughts and feelings.  Things will happen, emotions will come and go, but you don't need to be defined by them.  Stay detached and observe without getting swept away.

**Original Translation:**
Just as the wind carries scents from various objects, so does the embodied soul carry the mind's qualities—though he remains detached, unaffected, just as a gust of wind doesn’t affect the sky.

I'm starting my own business and I'm terrified of failure. I'm constantly checking social media for competitor updates and get really down when I see others succeeding. How can I stay focused and motivated without letting success and failure completely control my emotions and affect my well-being?

समदुःखसुखः स्वस्थः समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः |
तुल्यप्रियाप्रियो धीरस्तुल्यनिन्दात्मसंस्तुतिः ||१४-२४|| (Chapter 14, Verse 24)

**Simple Meaning:**
A truly wise person doesn't get too excited by good times or too upset by bad times. They accept whatever happens with a calm and balanced mind, and don't get bothered by praise or criticism. This mental steadiness is key to finding inner peace and freedom.

**Original Translation:**
The wise person who is equipoised in both happiness and distress, who is tolerant, free from envy, and who is steady in both success and failure, is certainly eligible for liberation.

I'm starting a new business and I'm already stressed about potential competition and negative reviews. Some days I feel on top of the world with early successes, other days I'm crushed by setbacks. How do I stay mentally balanced and focused on my work without letting these ups and downs completely control my emotions and well-being?

मानापमानयोस्तुल्यस्तुल्यो मित्रारिपक्षयोः |
सर्वारम्भपरित्यागी गुणातीतः स उच्यते ||१४-२५|| (Chapter 14, Verse 25)

**Simple Meaning:**
True freedom comes from letting go of emotional reactions to what happens to you. Don't get too high with praise or too low with criticism. Treat everyone equally – friends and foes alike – and don't get caught up in good times or bad, success or failure. When you're not defined by these things, you reach a higher state of being.

**Original Translation:**
One who is equal to friends and enemies, who is equipoised in honor and dishonor, heat and cold, happiness and distress, fame and infamy, who is always free from contaminating association, who is transcendental to all these material qualities, is indeed eligible for liberation.

I'm constantly stressed about work, relationships, and just life in general. I try to do the right thing, but I always seem to fall back into old patterns of anxiety and negativity. I know logically what I *should* do, but it's hard to stay focused and positive. How can I find a consistent inner peace and stop letting these everyday problems control my happiness?

मां च योऽव्यभिचारेण भक्तियोगेन सेवते |
स गुणान्समतीत्यैतान्ब्रह्मभूयाय कल्पते ||१४-२६|| (Chapter 14, Verse 26)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you dedicate yourself completely to doing good with unwavering devotion – no matter what happens – you'll rise above the usual ups and downs of life and connect with a deeper reality, the ultimate truth or Brahman.

**Original Translation:**
One who engages in full devotional service, unfailing in all circumstances, at once transcends the modes of material nature and thus comes to the level of Brahman.

I'm constantly chasing the next big thing – a better job, a bigger house, more money – hoping it will finally make me happy. But the happiness never lasts, and I always end up wanting something else. How do I find a happiness that's truly fulfilling and doesn't depend on external things?

ब्रह्मणो हि प्रतिष्ठाहममृतस्याव्ययस्य च |
शाश्वतस्य च धर्मस्य सुखस्यैकान्तिकस्य च ||१४-२७|| (Chapter 14, Verse 27)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna explains He is the foundation of everything unchanging and truly real – the ultimate reality (Brahman) that represents eternal happiness and never fades away. He’s the source and support of lasting peace and joy, beyond all temporary things.

**Original Translation:**
And I am the basis of the impersonal Brahman, which is immortal, imperishable and eternal and is the constitutional position of ultimate happiness.

I'm constantly torn between wanting to be healthy and productive (goodness), chasing exciting but often impractical opportunities (passion), and just wanting to relax and avoid effort (darkness). It feels like these three forces are always battling inside me, making it hard to focus and make good decisions. How can I understand these different impulses and find a way to live more harmoniously?

ॐ तत्सदिति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु
ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे
गुणत्रयविभागयोगो नाम चतुर्दशोऽध्यायः ||१४|| (Chapter 14, Verse 28)

**Simple Meaning:**
This chapter, the fourteenth of the Bhagavad Gita, is called 'The Yoga of the Division of the Three Gunas'. It explores how everything in the world – and even we ourselves – is influenced by three qualities or energies: goodness, passion, and darkness. Understanding these qualities helps us navigate life and move towards a more balanced and fulfilling existence.

**Original Translation:**
Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

I'm feeling incredibly overwhelmed by the complexities of life – all the different people, species, and experiences. It feels like so much chaos! Where does all this diversity come from? Is there a common origin to everything, or is it just random?

मम योनिर्महद् ब्रह्म तस्मिन्गर्भं दधाम्यहम् |
सम्भवः सर्वभूतानां ततो भवति भारत ||१४-३|| (Chapter 14, Verse 3)

**Simple Meaning:**
The entire universe and everything in it comes from a single source, which is Brahman.  I, Krishna, am involved in the process of creation, essentially 'seeding' Brahman to allow all life to be born. Think of it like the fundamental building block from which everything arises.

**Original Translation:**
The total material substance, called Brahman, is the source of birth, and it is that Brahman that I impregnate, making possible the births of all living beings, O son of Bharata.

I'm really struggling with the idea of where all life comes from. It feels overwhelming to think about everything existing, and where it all began. Sometimes I feel lost when thinking about the natural world, and how everything is interconnected. How does Krishna's role as the 'seed-giving father' help me understand the origin and connection of all living things and find peace with it?

सर्वयोनिषु कौन्तेय मूर्तयः सम्भवन्ति याः |
तासां ब्रह्म महद्योनिरहं बीजप्रदः पिता ||१४-४|| (Chapter 14, Verse 4)

**Simple Meaning:**
Every living thing you see – plants, animals, humans – comes into being through the natural world. Krishna, as the divine source, is like the original creator, providing the life force that makes all of this possible. He's the source of all existence.

**Original Translation:**
It should be understood that all species of life, O son of Kuntī, are made possible by birth in this material nature, and that I am the seed-giving father.

I'm constantly struggling with feeling motivated and productive sometimes, and completely drained and apathetic at other times. One day I'm excited about my goals, the next I can barely get out of bed. It feels like my energy and outlook are all over the place. How can I understand these ups and downs and find more balance in my life, instead of being a slave to my moods?

सत्त्वं रजस्तम इति गुणाः प्रकृतिसम्भवाः |
निबध्नन्ति महाबाहो देहे देहिनमव्ययम् ||१४-५|| (Chapter 14, Verse 5)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything in the world – and everything we experience – is shaped by three qualities: purity (goodness), activity/desire (passion), and darkness/inertia (ignorance). When our soul connects with the world, it gets influenced by these qualities, which causes us to feel attached and experience different emotions and situations. These qualities aren’t permanent, but they can feel very real while we're experiencing them.

**Original Translation:**
Material nature consists of three modes – goodness, passion and ignorance. When the eternal living entity comes in contact with nature, O mighty-armed Arjuna, he becomes conditioned by these modes.

I've been working really hard on self-improvement – reading, meditating, eating healthy, and I feel really good about my progress. But I'm starting to feel anxious if I miss a day of my routine, or if I encounter setbacks. I'm afraid of losing the 'good' feelings I've worked so hard to achieve. Is it possible to be *too* focused on being 'good' or 'enlightened', and how do I find balance?

तत्र सत्त्वं निर्मलत्वात्प्रकाशकमनामयम् |
सुखसङ्गेन बध्नाति ज्ञानसङ्गेन चानघ ||१४-६|| (Chapter 14, Verse 6)

**Simple Meaning:**
Goodness (Sattva) is the purest state of mind – it brings clarity and frees you from negativity. However, even good things can hold you back if you get too attached to them, like getting overly focused on happiness or knowledge and losing sight of a bigger picture.

**Original Translation:**
O sinless one, the mode of goodness, being purer than the others, is illuminating, and it frees one from all sinful reactions. Those situated in that mode become conditioned by a sense of happiness and knowledge.

I'm constantly working towards my next promotion, always chasing a bigger salary and better title. I feel like I can *never* relax or be happy because I'm always worried about the next step. Even when I achieve something, the feeling doesn't last long because I immediately start focusing on what's next. How can I find peace and stop feeling so trapped in this endless pursuit?

रजो रागात्मकं विद्धि तृष्णासङ्गसमुद्भवम् |
तन्निबध्नाति कौन्तेय कर्मसङ्गेन देहिनम् ||१४-७|| (Chapter 14, Verse 7)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse explains that when we're driven by constant cravings and desires, we get stuck in a cycle of doing things just to get results. The more we chase these temporary pleasures, the more attached we become to the outcomes of our actions, and the harder it becomes to break free from this cycle. Basically, desire leads to attachment and keeps us bound to worldly activities.

**Original Translation:**
The mode of passion is born of unlimited desires and longings, O son of Kuntī, and because of this the embodied living entity is bound to material fruitive actions.

I've been feeling completely unmotivated lately. I just want to binge-watch shows and sleep all day, even though I know I have important things I need to do. I feel overwhelmed and like I'm stuck in a rut. How can I break this cycle of laziness and find the energy to start living my life again?

तमस्त्वज्ञानजं विद्धि मोहनं सर्वदेहिनाम् |
प्रमादालस्यनिद्राभिस्तन्निबध्नाति भारत ||१४-८|| (Chapter 14, Verse 8)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that ignorance creates a kind of fog in our minds, making us confused and stuck. This confusion leads to laziness, not caring enough, and just wanting to sleep or escape reality, which keeps us from growing and finding happiness. It’s like a cycle that traps us.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Bharata, know that the mode of darkness, born of ignorance, is the delusion of all embodied living entities. The results of this mode are madness, indolence and sleep, which bind the conditioned soul.

I've been working crazy hours at my job trying to get a promotion, and even though I'm 'successful' by external standards, I feel completely burnt out, anxious, and disconnected from my friends and family. I'm constantly stressed about the next task or deadline. How can I find a better balance and figure out what's *really* important to me instead of just chasing achievements?

सत्त्वं सुखे सञ्जयति रजः कर्मणि भारत |
ज्ञानमावृत्य तु तमः प्रमादे सञ्जयत्युत ||१४-९|| (Chapter 14, Verse 9)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse explains how different qualities affect us. Goodness leads to happiness, passion drives us to work and get results, and ignorance makes us confused and careless, blocking our wisdom. Basically, our state of mind impacts how we experience life – a clear mind leads to joy, while a clouded one leads to problems.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Bharata, the mode of goodness conditions one to happiness; passion conditions one to fruitive action; and ignorance, covering one’s knowledge, binds one to madness.

I’m feeling really lost and overwhelmed with all the different paths people tell me to find happiness – religion, career, relationships, possessions… It feels like everyone has a different answer, and nothing truly satisfies me. I'm starting to doubt if there even *is* a single source of truth or meaning. How can I navigate all this confusion and find a sense of grounding when everything feels so disconnected?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
ऊर्ध्वमूलमधःशाखमश्वत्थं प्राहुरव्ययम् |
छन्दांसि यस्य पर्णानि यस्तं वेद स वेदवित् ||१५-१|| (Chapter 15, Verse 1)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse describes the universe as a giant, upside-down banyan tree representing our interconnected existence and the cycle of life. The roots go upwards, symbolizing our connection to the divine source, and the branches spread downwards representing our actions and experiences in the world. Understanding this whole system – how everything is connected and comes from the same source – is true knowledge.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: It is said that there is an imperishable banyan tree that has its roots upward and its branches down and whose leaves are the Vedic hymns. One who knows this tree is the knower of the Vedas.

My grandfather recently passed away, and I'm really struggling with the idea of where he is now. I keep getting caught up in grief and can't seem to accept it.  Everyone around me is offering different opinions, but none of them feel right. How can I find peace and understand what happened to him, and what happens after we die, instead of getting stuck in this pain?

उत्क्रामन्तं स्थितं वापि भुञ्जानं वा गुणान्वितम् |
विमूढा नानुपश्यन्ति पश्यन्ति ज्ञानचक्षुषः ||१५-१०|| (Chapter 15, Verse 10)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who don't understand the nature of reality can't grasp what happens after death or how our experiences are shaped by our desires and attachments. But those with wisdom can see the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth and how our qualities influence our lives.

**Original Translation:**
The foolish cannot understand how a living entity can quit his body, nor can they understand what sort of body he enjoys under the spell of the modes of nature. But one whose eyes are trained in knowledge can see all this.

I've been trying so hard to find happiness through my job, possessions, and relationships, but nothing seems to truly satisfy me. I'm constantly chasing the next thing, but I still feel empty. Is it possible I'm looking in the wrong places, and how do I actually start 'seeing' what's really going on?

यतन्तो योगिनश्चैनं पश्यन्त्यात्मन्यवस्थितम् |
यतन्तोऽप्यकृतात्मानो नैनं पश्यन्त्यचेतसः ||१५-११|| (Chapter 15, Verse 11)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who are sincerely trying to understand themselves and connect with their inner truth can clearly see the deeper reality of life. However, those who haven't focused on self-discovery and are lost in their thoughts and desires struggle to understand, even if they try.

**Original Translation:**
The endeavoring transcendentalists who are situated in self-realization can see all this clearly. But those whose minds are not developed and who are not situated in self-realization cannot see what is taking place, though they may try.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed by all the different sources of innovation and creativity in the tech world. Every day there's a new app, a new company, a new idea. It's hard to see how anything truly original exists. How can I remember that all these ideas, even though they seem distinct, might actually come from the same underlying source of inspiration and potential?

यदादित्यगतं तेजो जगद्भासयतेऽखिलम् |
यच्चन्द्रमसि यच्चाग्नौ तत्तेजो विद्धि मामकम् ||१५-१२|| (Chapter 15, Verse 12)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything that shines – the sun's brightness, the moon's glow, even the heat from a fire – all that energy and light ultimately comes from the same divine source. It's a reminder that a single power is behind all the brilliance we see in the world.

**Original Translation:**
The splendor of the sun, which dissipates the darkness of this whole world, comes from Me. And the splendor of the moon and the splendor of fire are also from Me.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed with all the problems in the world – climate change, food shortages, political unrest. It feels like everything is falling apart. How can I find hope and remember that things are fundamentally okay when so much seems broken and unsustainable?

गामाविश्य च भूतानि धारयाम्यहमोजसा |
पुष्णामि चौषधीः सर्वाः सोमो भूत्वा रसात्मकः ||१५-१३|| (Chapter 15, Verse 13)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna says He's the energy that keeps everything in the universe functioning – He's present in all planets, giving them the power to stay in place. He also provides nourishment to all plants, like the moon gives water. Essentially, He's the life-sustaining force behind all creation.

**Original Translation:**
I enter into each planet, and by My energy they stay in orbit. I become the moon and thereby supply the juice of life to all vegetables.

I'm really stressed about my diet and constantly worried about 'good' and 'bad' foods. I feel guilty after eating certain things and obsessed with getting everything 'right'. Is it possible that this worry about food is missing the bigger picture, and how can I find a more balanced relationship with what I eat?

अहं वैश्वानरो भूत्वा प्राणिनां देहमाश्रितः |
प्राणापानसमायुक्तः पचाम्यन्नं चतुर्विधम् ||१५-१४|| (Chapter 15, Verse 14)

**Simple Meaning:**
The divine energy is present within every living being as the power of digestion. It's how we take in food and transform it into life-sustaining energy, working with our breath to keep us going. Essentially, the power that fuels our bodies is a manifestation of the divine.

**Original Translation:**
I am the fire of digestion in the bodies of all living entities, and I join with the air of life, outgoing and incoming, to digest the four kinds of foodstuff.

I'm feeling really overwhelmed by all the different information and self-help advice out there – from books, podcasts, therapy, and online courses. It's hard to know what's true and what actually works. Sometimes I feel like I'm remembering things that aren’t helpful, and other times I completely forget valuable lessons. How can I find clarity and inner wisdom amidst all this noise and confusion?

सर्वस्य चाहं हृदि सन्निविष्टो
मत्तः स्मृतिर्ज्ञानमपोहनञ्च |
वेदैश्च सर्वैरहमेव वेद्यो
वेदान्तकृद्वेदविदेव चाहम् ||१५-१५|| (Chapter 15, Verse 15)

**Simple Meaning:**
The divine presence exists within everyone – in your thoughts, memories, and understanding. Everything you remember, everything you learn, and even what you forget, all originates from that same source. All scriptures and knowledge ultimately point to understanding this one ultimate reality, and it is the source of all Vedic wisdom.

**Original Translation:**
I am seated in everyone’s heart, and from Me come remembrance, knowledge and forgetfulness. By all the Vedas, I am to be known. Indeed, I am the compiler of Vedānta, and I am the knower of the Vedas.

I'm really struggling with the loss of my grandfather. It feels like a huge part of me is gone, and I can't stop grieving. How can I cope with this pain, knowing that everything changes and eventually ends, but still feel connected to him and find peace?

द्वाविमौ पुरुषौ लोके क्षरश्चाक्षर एव च |
क्षरः सर्वाणि भूतानि कूटस्थोऽक्षर उच्यते ||१५-१६|| (Chapter 15, Verse 16)

**Simple Meaning:**
There are two types of beings: those who are temporary and changing (like us in our physical bodies), and those who are eternal and unchanging (the spiritual self). Everything we experience in the world is temporary, but our true self is permanent.

**Original Translation:**
There are two classes of beings, the fallible and the infallible. In the material world every living entity is fallible, and in the spiritual world every living entity is called infallible.

I feel like everything in my life is temporary – relationships change, jobs come and go, even my own body is constantly changing. It's hard to find something solid to believe in or rely on. How can I find peace knowing that everything is so unstable and constantly shifting?

उत्तमः पुरुषस्त्वन्यः परमात्मेत्युधाहृतः |
यो लोकत्रयमाविश्य बिभर्त्यव्यय ईश्वरः ||१५-१७|| (Chapter 15, Verse 17)

**Simple Meaning:**
There's a higher reality beyond everything we experience – the Supreme Soul, also known as the Lord. This ultimate reality exists within and beyond the three worlds (physical, subtle, and causal) and is what supports and sustains everything. It's the unchanging foundation of existence.

**Original Translation:**
Besides these two, there is the greatest living personality, the Supreme Soul, the imperishable Lord Himself, who has entered the three worlds and is maintaining them.

I'm feeling stuck trying to find meaning in life. Everything seems temporary and conditional – relationships change, achievements fade, even my own values feel uncertain. I’ve tried following different philosophies and rules, but nothing provides lasting peace. How can I find something truly stable and fulfilling that isn't subject to the constant changes and limitations of the world?

यस्मात्क्षरमतीतोऽहमक्षरादपि चोत्तमः |
अतोऽस्मि लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः ||१५-१८|| (Chapter 15, Verse 18)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna is explaining that He's beyond all limitations – He's not bound by the rules that apply to the temporary world or even to eternal principles. He is the ultimate reality, recognized as the Supreme Person throughout existence, both in practical life and in spiritual scriptures like the Vedas.

**Original Translation:**
Because I am transcendental, beyond both the fallible and the infallible, and because I am the greatest, I am celebrated both in the world and in the Vedas as that Supreme Person.

I'm constantly stressed about making the 'right' choices in life – career, relationships, everything. I overthink all the possibilities and feel paralyzed by fear of regret. I've tried many self-help techniques, but nothing seems to stick. How can I find peace and clarity amidst all this confusion, and actually trust my decisions without constantly second-guessing myself?

यो मामेवमसम्मूढो जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम् |
स सर्वविद्भजति मां सर्वभावेन भारत ||१५-१९|| (Chapter 15, Verse 19)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you truly understand and believe in God (or a higher power) without any doubt, you'll naturally devote yourself to Him fully and understand everything in life better. It's about complete faith leading to complete understanding and love.

**Original Translation:**
Whoever knows Me as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, without doubting, is the knower of everything. He therefore engages himself in full devotional service to Me, O son of Bharata.

I'm feeling stuck in a cycle of constantly chasing the next promotion or material possession, hoping it will make me happy, but it never does. I just end up wanting more. It's exhausting and feels meaningless. How do I break free from this cycle and find lasting contentment instead of always needing something external to feel okay?

अधश्चोर्ध्वं प्रसृतास्तस्य शाखा
गुणप्रवृद्धा विषयप्रवालाः |
अधश्च मूलान्यनुसन्ततानि
कर्मानुबन्धीनि मनुष्यलोके ||१५-२|| (Chapter 15, Verse 2)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse describes life as a giant tree. Its roots are our actions (karma) and desires, the trunk represents our current life, the branches are our experiences, and the leaves are the things we enjoy. The tree grows because of our basic qualities (like energy, calmness, or passion). Basically, our lives are shaped by what we do and the kinds of people we are, and it keeps growing based on those things.

**Original Translation:**
The branches of this tree extend downward and upward, nourished by the three modes of material nature. The twigs are the objects of the senses. This tree also has roots going down, and these are bound to the fruitive actions of human society.

I've been working towards a specific career goal for years, but lately, I feel lost and unsure if it's actually what I want. I'm questioning all my efforts and feeling really discouraged. How can I find clarity and know if I'm on the right path, and if not, how do I find fulfillment in changing course?

इति गुह्यतमं शास्त्रमिदमुक्तं मयानघ |
एतद्बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान्स्यात्कृतकृत्यश्च भारत ||१५-२०|| (Chapter 15, Verse 20)

**Simple Meaning:**
This is the most important teaching from the ancient scriptures, and I'm sharing it with you now. If you truly understand this wisdom, you'll gain clarity and find fulfillment in everything you do.

**Original Translation:**
This is the most confidential part of the Vedic scriptures, O sinless one, and it is disclosed now by Me. Whoever understands this will become wise, and his endeavors will know perfection.

I'm feeling really lost and disconnected lately. It feels like everyone is chasing different things, and I don’t know what my purpose is. I'm starting to question everything I thought was important. How can I find a sense of grounding and understand what truly matters when everything feels so uncertain and temporary?

ॐ तत्सदिति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु
ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे श्रीकृष्णार्जुन संवादे
पुरुषोत्तमयोगो नाम पञ्चदशोऽध्यायः ||१५|| (Chapter 15, Verse 21)

**Simple Meaning:**
This chapter, the fifteenth of the Bhagavad Gita, is called the Yoga of the Supreme Person. It explores the highest truths about reality and our connection to the divine, presented as a conversation between Krishna and Arjuna. It's about understanding the ultimate source of everything and our place within it.

**Original Translation:**
Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

I've been working at the same job for 10 years, even though it makes me miserable. I'm scared to leave because it's familiar and provides security, but I feel like it's holding me back from pursuing what I really want. How can I find the courage to detach from this situation and make a change, even though it feels so deeply rooted in my life?

न रूपमस्येह तथोपलभ्यते
नान्तो न चादिर्न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा |
अश्वत्थमेनं सुविरूढमूलं
असङ्गशस्त्रेण दृढेन छित्त्वा ||१५-३|| (Chapter 15, Verse 3)

**Simple Meaning:**
This world and our attachments to it are like a huge, ancient tree with roots all around us – we can’t see its beginning or end, and it’s really hard to get rid of it. But if we’re determined and let go of our attachments, we can break free from its hold.

**Original Translation:**
The real form of this tree cannot be perceived in this world. No one can understand where it ends, where it begins, or where its foundation is. But with determination one must cut down this strongly rooted tree with the weapon of detachment.

I feel stuck in a pattern of always chasing the next big thing – a new job, a better relationship, more possessions – but nothing ever feels truly fulfilling. I get a temporary high, then I'm searching again. How can I find lasting peace and break free from this endless cycle of wanting, and find something truly permanent to dedicate my life to?

ततः पदं तत्परिमार्गितव्यं
यस्मिन्गता न निवर्तन्ति भूयः |
तमेव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये |
यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी ||१५-४|| (Chapter 15, Verse 4)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse tells us to find the ultimate goal – a state of being free from repeated birth and death. Once you reach that place, you don't come back to this cycle. To get there, fully surrender to the Supreme Godhead, who is the source of everything and has always been there.

**Original Translation:**
Thereafter, one must seek that place from which, having gone, one never returns, and there surrender to that Supreme Personality of Godhead from whom everything has begun and in whom everything is abiding since time immemorial.

I've been chasing success and validation my whole life, constantly worried about what others think and comparing myself to them. It feels like a never-ending cycle of temporary happiness and disappointment. I'm starting to feel empty despite achieving many of my goals. How can I break free from this cycle and find a more meaningful, lasting sense of peace and fulfillment?

निर्मानमोहा जितसङ्गदोषा
अध्यात्मनित्या विनिवृत्तकामाः |
द्वन्द्वैर्विमुक्ताः सुखदुःखसंज्ञैर्-
गच्छन्त्यमूढाः पदमव्ययं तत् ||१५-५|| (Chapter 15, Verse 5)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who aren't caught up in ego, illusions, or unhealthy relationships, who understand their true spiritual nature, have overcome selfish desires, and aren't bothered by ups and downs in life, they find lasting peace and reach a state of eternal happiness.

**Original Translation:**
Those who are free from false prestige, illusion and false association, who understand the eternal, who are done with material lust, who are freed from the dualities of happiness and distress, and who, unbewildered, know how to surrender unto the Supreme Person attain to that eternal kingdom.

I've been chasing success – better jobs, more money, a bigger house – but nothing seems to truly satisfy me. Even when I achieve something, the feeling doesn't last. I feel like I'm on a hamster wheel. Is there a state of being where I can find lasting peace and fulfillment, and not constantly need 'more'?

न तद्भासयते सूर्यो न शशाङ्को न पावकः |
यद्गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते तद्धाम परमं मम ||१५-६|| (Chapter 15, Verse 6)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse describes Krishna's ultimate reality – a place beyond our everyday understanding of space and time. It's not reachable through physical means like light or energy. Once someone reaches this state of oneness with Krishna, they don't come back to the cycle of birth and death.

**Original Translation:**
That supreme abode of Mine is not illumined by the sun or moon, nor by fire or electricity. Those who reach it never return to this material world.

I feel completely overwhelmed by my desires and emotions – I'm constantly chasing after things that never truly satisfy me. It's like my own mind is working against me, making me unhappy. How can I find peace when I'm always battling these internal struggles?

ममैवांशो जीवलोके जीवभूतः सनातनः |
मनःषष्ठानीन्द्रियाणि प्रकृतिस्थानि कर्षति ||१५-७|| (Chapter 15, Verse 7)

**Simple Meaning:**
Every living being is a tiny part of the divine, an eternal spark of God within us. Because we're caught up in worldly life, we constantly struggle with our senses and thoughts, creating difficulties for ourselves.

**Original Translation:**
The living entities in this conditioned world are My eternal fragmental parts. Due to conditioned life, they are struggling very hard with the six senses, which include the mind.

I've been feeling really lost after my grandmother passed away. I keep thinking about all the things she taught me and the kind of person she was.
