This is the introduction to the Bhagavad Gita, a conversation between Krishna and Arjuna that explores the science of self-realization and the path to liberation. It sets the stage for a profound discussion about life, duty, and how to find peace amidst challenges.

**Original Translation:**
Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

I'm starting a new business and feel really intimidated because all my competitors are already successful and seem really capable. How do I stay motivated and face the challenge when it feels like everyone around me is a strong rival?

धृष्टकेतुश्चेकितानः काशिराजश्च वीर्यवान् |
पुरुजित्कुन्तिभोजश्च शैब्यश्च नरपुंगवः ||१-५|| (Chapter 1, Verse 5)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse is listing some of the many strong and brave warriors who are fighting on the opposing side. It highlights that the battle isn't against weak opponents, but against skilled and powerful individuals.

**Original Translation:**
There are also great heroic, powerful fighters like Dhṛṣṭaketu, Cekitāna, Kāśirāja, Purujit, Kuntibhoja and Śaibya.

I'm starting a new team at work, and I'm feeling really overwhelmed. I'm worried I don't have enough skilled people to succeed. Even though I have some talented individuals, I'm doubting if they're strong enough to face the challenges ahead. How can I approach this feeling and have confidence in the team I've assembled, knowing everyone has their own strengths?

युधामन्युश्च विक्रान्त उत्तमौजाश्च वीर्यवान् |
सौभद्रो द्रौपदेयाश्च सर्व एव महारथाः ||१-६|| (Chapter 1, Verse 6)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna has many incredible allies fighting with him – skilled warriors like Yudhāmanyu, Uttamaujā, and the sons of Draupadi. These aren't just any fighters; they are exceptionally brave and powerful chariot warriors, capable of turning the tide of battle.

**Original Translation:**
There are the mighty Yudhāmanyu, the very powerful Uttamaujā, the son of Subhadrā and the sons of Draupadī. All these warriors are great chariot fighters.

I'm starting a new project at work and have a lot of talented people on my team. I want to quickly get the new manager up to speed on who the real go-to people are – the ones I rely on the most. How do I best present this information to them so they understand who the key players are and can work effectively with everyone?

अस्माकं तु विशिष्टा ये तान्निबोध द्विजोत्तम |
नायका मम सैन्यस्य संज्ञार्थं तान्ब्रवीमि ते ||१-७|| (Chapter 1, Verse 7)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna is telling Krishna that he wants to introduce the most important leaders from his army, the ones who are most skilled and capable. He's essentially giving Krishna a rundown of his key personnel.

**Original Translation:**
But for your information, O best of the brāhmaṇas, let me tell you about the captains who are especially qualified to lead my military force.

I'm starting a new project at work, and honestly, the other team leading the competing initiative is *really* good. They have a history of successful launches and a lot of talented people. It's intimidating, and I'm worried we won't be able to compete. How do I approach this knowing they are formidable opponents, and not get discouraged before we even start?

भवान्भीष्मश्च कर्णश्च कृपश्च समितिञ्जयः |
अश्वत्थामा विकर्णश्च सौमदत्तिस्तथैव च ||१-८|| (Chapter 1, Verse 8)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse lists incredibly skilled and powerful warriors on the opposing side of the battle. It acknowledges that the opposing team isn't weak – they are full of heroes known for their strength and success in combat. It sets the stage for a truly challenging war.

**Original Translation:**
There are personalities like you, Bhīṣma, Karṇa, Kṛpa, Aśvatthāmā, Vikarṇa and the son of Somadatta called Bhūriśravā, who are always victorious in battle.

I'm leading a team on a really challenging project at work, and I feel immense pressure because I think everything will fall apart if *I* don't personally handle every critical task. I feel like it's all on my shoulders. How do I trust others to step up and share the responsibility, knowing they might not do things *exactly* how I would?

अन्ये च बहवः शूरा मदर्थे त्यक्तजीविताः |
नानाशस्त्रप्रहरणाः सर्वे युद्धविशारदाः ||१-९|| (Chapter 1, Verse 9)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna isn't alone in his duty. Many other brave and skilled warriors are ready to fight and even sacrifice their lives for the same cause. This shows that dedication and support exist beyond any single person's commitment.

**Original Translation:**
There are many other heroes who are prepared to lay down their lives for my sake. All of them are well equipped with different kinds of weapons, and all are experienced in military science.

My close friend just started working for a company that's doing something I deeply disagree with ethically. I feel terrible for them, and now I’m starting to question our friendship. I don’t want to lose them, but supporting them feels wrong. How do I navigate this difficult situation without causing further pain or compromising my own values?

सञ्जय उवाच |
तं तथा कृपयाविष्टमश्रुपूर्णाकुलेक्षणम् |
विषीदन्तमिदं वाक्यमुवाच मधुसूदनः ||२-१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 1)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, a great warrior, was overcome with sadness and grief when faced with fighting his own relatives in the upcoming war. Seeing his distress, Krishna, his friend and guide, decided to offer him wisdom and guidance.

**Original Translation:**
Sañjaya said: Seeing Arjuna full of compassion, his mind depressed, his eyes full of tears, Madhusūdana, Kṛṣṇa, spoke the following words.

My family is incredibly divided over political issues, and it’s causing huge tension at every gathering. I feel heartbroken and stuck in the middle, not wanting to take sides but also not wanting to lose my relationships. How can I navigate this situation without adding to the conflict and still stay true to myself?

तमुवाच हृषीकेशः प्रहसन्निव भारत |
सेनयोरुभयोर्मध्ये विषीदन्तमिदं वचः ||२-१०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 10)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna was overwhelmed with sadness seeing his relatives and friends on both sides of the battlefield. Right then, Krishna, with a gentle smile, started to speak words of wisdom to comfort and guide him.

**Original Translation:**
O descendant of Bharata, at that time Kṛṣṇa, smiling, in the midst of both the armies, spoke the following words to the grief-stricken Arjuna.

My grandmother recently passed away, and even though she lived a long and fulfilling life, I'm incredibly upset and constantly thinking about her. My friends say I should move on, but I can't shake this feeling of loss. How can I cope with this grief when everyone tells me I shouldn't be so sad because she had a good life?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
अशोच्यानन्वशोचस्त्वं प्रज्ञावादांश्च भाषसे |
गतासूनगतासूंश्च नानुशोचन्ति पण्डिताः ||२-११|| (Chapter 2, Verse 11)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna points out to Arjuna that he's getting upset over something that doesn't deserve sadness – people coming and going is just a natural part of life. Wise people don't waste their energy grieving over those who have lived or those who are currently alive, understanding that everything is temporary.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: While speaking learned words, you are mourning for what is not worthy of grief. Those who are wise lament neither for the living nor for the dead.

My grandmother passed away recently, and I'm really struggling with the grief and the feeling that she's completely gone. It feels like a part of me is missing. It’s hard to accept that she won't be here anymore in the way I knew her. How can I find peace knowing that this loss is permanent and how do I cope with the feeling that she’s simply…gone?

न त्वेवाहं जातु नासं न त्वं नेमे जनाधिपाः |
न चैव न भविष्यामः सर्वे वयमतः परम् ||२-१२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 12)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that existence itself is eternal. You, me, and everyone around us have always existed in some form, and will continue to exist in some form.  Death isn't an end, but a change in form. It's like energy transforming, not disappearing.

**Original Translation:**
Never was there a time when I did not exist, nor you, nor all these kings; nor in the future shall any of us cease to be.

My grandmother recently passed away, and I'm really struggling with the grief and the feeling that she's completely gone. It's hard to accept that she won't be here physically anymore. How can I cope with this loss and find peace knowing that everything changes?

देहिनोऽस्मिन्यथा देहे कौमारं यौवनं जरा |
तथा देहान्तरप्राप्तिर्धीरस्तत्र न मुह्यति ||२-१३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 13)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like we change clothes throughout our lives – from baby clothes to adult sizes – the soul changes bodies throughout its journey. A wise person understands this cycle and doesn't fear or get confused by death, knowing it’s just another change.

**Original Translation:**
As the embodied soul continuously passes, in this body, from boyhood to youth to old age, the soul similarly passes into another body at death. A sober person is not bewildered by such a change.

I just lost my job after years of dedication, and I'm feeling incredibly anxious and devastated. I'm also worried about how I'll pay my bills. It feels like my whole world is falling apart. How can I deal with this emotional turmoil and find a sense of peace when everything feels so uncertain and painful?

मात्रास्पर्शास्तु कौन्तेय शीतोष्णसुखदुःखदाः |
आगमापायिनोऽनित्यास्तांस्तितिक्षस्व भारत ||२-१४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 14)

**Simple Meaning:**
Life has ups and downs – good times and bad. They come and go, just like seasons change. Don't get too attached to pleasure or too upset by pain, because both are temporary and a natural part of existence. The key is to stay balanced and accept them as they come.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Kuntī, the nonpermanent appearance of happiness and distress, and their disappearance in due course, are like the appearance and disappearance of winter and summer seasons. They arise from sense perception, O scion of Bharata, and one must learn to tolerate them without being disturbed.

I just lost my job after 10 years and am completely devastated. On the other hand, my friend got a huge promotion and is over the moon. I feel so down and it's hard to even get out of bed. How do I deal with these extreme ups and downs without letting them completely control my life and happiness?

यं हि न व्यथयन्त्येते पुरुषं पुरुषर्षभ |
समदुःखसुखं धीरं सोऽमृतत्वाय कल्पते ||२-१५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 15)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, someone who doesn't get too excited by good times or too upset by bad times – someone who remains calm and balanced – is the one who's truly on the path to freedom and lasting peace.

**Original Translation:**
O best among men [Arjuna], the person who is not disturbed by happiness and distress and is steady in both is certainly eligible for liberation.

My grandfather recently passed away, and I'm really struggling with the grief. It feels like a huge part of me is gone. Everyone keeps telling me to 'move on,' but it’s hard. How can I cope with this loss and find peace when everything feels so impermanent?

नासतो विद्यते भावो नाभावो विद्यते सतः |
उभयोरपि दृष्टोऽन्तस्त्वनयोस्तत्त्वदर्शिभिः ||२-१६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 16)

**Simple Meaning:**
Real wisdom comes from understanding that things change – bodies are temporary and eventually disappear, but the true self, the soul, always exists. Wise people see this truth by looking closely at the nature of reality.

**Original Translation:**
Those who are seers of the truth have concluded that of the nonexistent [the material body] there is no endurance and of the eternal [the soul] there is no change. This they have concluded by studying the nature of both.

My grandmother recently passed away and I'm feeling incredibly lost and sad. It's hard to reconcile her physical absence with the love I still feel for her. How can I cope with this grief knowing that something of her must still *be* somewhere, even if I can't see her?

अविनाशि तु तद्विद्धि येन सर्वमिदं ततम् |
विनाशमव्ययस्यास्य न कश्चित्कर्तुमर्हति ||२-१७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 17)

**Simple Meaning:**
The essence of who you are – your true self, or soul – is eternal and cannot be destroyed. It exists throughout your entire being and continues to exist even after the body dies.

**Original Translation:**
That which pervades the entire body you should know to be indestructible. No one is able to destroy that imperishable soul.

I'm facing a really difficult career decision. Leaving my stable job feels incredibly risky and I'm scared of failing and losing everything I've worked for. It's paralyzing me. How do I overcome this fear and make a decision based on what I *should* do, instead of just what feels safest?

अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः |
अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत ||२-१८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 18)

**Simple Meaning:**
Your body will eventually die, but your true self – the soul – is eternal and cannot be destroyed. So don't be afraid of death in battle or life. Focus on your duty and keep fighting, because your soul will live on.

**Original Translation:**
The material body of the indestructible, immeasurable and eternal living entity is sure to come to an end; therefore, fight, O descendant of Bharata.

My friend lost his job and is blaming himself for the company's failure, constantly saying he 'killed' his career. He's really down and feels like his life is over. How can I help him understand that this situation doesn’t define him and that he’s not responsible for something bigger than himself, and that he's not 'killing' his future, but just facing a setback?

य एनं वेत्ति हन्तारं यश्चैनं मन्यते हतम् |
उभौ तौ न विजानीतो नायं हन्ति न हन्यते ||२-१९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 19)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse explains that the true self, or soul, doesn't kill and can't be killed. Thinking of someone as being killed, or thinking of yourself as killing someone, is based on a misunderstanding of reality. It's a matter of perspective and recognizing the eternal nature of the soul, which is beyond birth and death.

**Original Translation:**
Neither he who thinks the living entity the slayer nor he who thinks it slain is in knowledge, for the self slays not nor is slain.

My friend just failed a really important exam and is now completely giving up on their dream career, saying it's pointless to even try anymore and they're feeling really ashamed and lost. How can I help them see past this temporary setback and not let it ruin their future?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
कुतस्त्वा कश्मलमिदं विषमे समुपस्थितम् |
अनार्यजुष्टमस्वर्ग्यमकीर्तिकरमर्जुन ||२-२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 2)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, where did this negativity come from? It doesn't suit someone like you who understands what's truly important. Dwelling on this will only bring you down and damage your reputation.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: My dear Arjuna, how have these impurities come upon you? They are not at all befitting a man who knows the value of life. They lead not to higher planets but to infamy.

My grandfather just passed away, and I'm really struggling with the grief. It feels like he's completely gone, and I keep thinking about all the memories we'll never make. How can I cope with this loss knowing everything feels so final, and is there a way to believe he's not truly 'gone'?

न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्
नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः |
अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो
न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ||२-२०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 20)

**Simple Meaning:**
The soul, your true self, never dies and was never born. It's always existed and will always exist. When the body dies, the soul doesn't – it simply changes forms.  It’s like changing clothes; the person wearing the clothes remains the same.

**Original Translation:**
For the soul there is neither birth nor death at any time. He has not come into being, does not come into being, and will not come into being. He is unborn, eternal, ever-existing and primeval. He is not slain when the body is slain.

My friend is really struggling with the guilt of having to lay off people at his company due to financial difficulties. He feels terrible even though he knows it's necessary for the business to survive. How can I help him reconcile his actions with his belief that everyone deserves respect and dignity, and that harming others is wrong?

वेदाविनाशिनं नित्यं य एनमजमव्ययम् |
कथं स पुरुषः पार्थ कं घातयति हन्ति कम् ||२-२१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 21)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you truly understand that the soul never dies, is always there, wasn't born, and can't be destroyed, then how can you possibly believe you're harming anyone by killing or getting killed? The true self is beyond death.

**Original Translation:**
O Pārtha, how can a person who knows that the soul is indestructible, eternal, unborn and immutable kill anyone or cause anyone to kill?

My grandmother recently passed away, and I'm really struggling with grief and the idea of her being 'gone' forever. It feels so final. This verse talks about changing bodies, but how can I use this idea to cope with the pain of losing someone I love, and understand where she is now?

वासांसि जीर्णानि यथा विहाय
नवानि गृह्णाति नरोऽपराणि |
तथा शरीराणि विहाय जीर्णा-
न्यन्यानि संयाति नवानि देही ||२-२२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 22)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like you change clothes when they get old or worn out, your soul keeps changing bodies throughout life. This isn't death, but simply a transition to a new form. It's a natural cycle of renewal.

**Original Translation:**
As a person puts on new garments, giving up old ones, the soul similarly accepts new material bodies, giving up the old and useless ones.

I'm going through a really tough breakup and feel completely broken. Everything feels ruined and I’m worried I’ll never recover from this pain. How can I find strength and remember who I am when I feel like a part of me has been destroyed?

नैनं छिन्दन्ति शस्त्राणि नैनं दहति पावकः |
न चैनं क्लेदयन्त्यापो न शोषयति मारुतः ||२-२३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 23)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse means the true self, your soul, is indestructible. It can’t be harmed or destroyed by anything physical in the world – weapons, fire, water, or even natural forces like wind. It’s eternal and unchanging.

**Original Translation:**
The soul can never be cut to pieces by any weapon, nor burned by fire, nor moistened by water, nor withered by the wind.

I'm going through a really tough breakup, and I feel like a part of me is completely shattered. Everything reminds me of them, and I'm scared I'll never feel whole again. How can I remember who *I* am when everything feels lost and broken? Is there a part of me that remains unchanged even through this pain?

अच्छेद्योऽयमदाह्योऽयमक्लेद्योऽशोष्य एव च |
नित्यः सर्वगतः स्थाणुरचलोऽयं सनातनः ||२-२४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 24)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse tells us that your true self – the soul – is fundamentally indestructible. It can't be harmed by anything physical, it doesn't get destroyed with the body, and it's always been and always will be. It's present everywhere and doesn't change.

**Original Translation:**
This individual soul is unbreakable and insoluble, and can be neither burned nor dried. He is everlasting, present everywhere, unchangeable, immovable and eternally the same.

My grandmother recently passed away, and I'm heartbroken. Everyone keeps saying 'she's in a better place,' but I miss her so much. It feels impossible to move on knowing she's gone. How can I cope with this loss when all I feel is sadness, knowing that everything about her is physically gone?

अव्यक्तोऽयमचिन्त्योऽयमविकार्योऽयमुच्यते |
तस्मादेवं विदित्वैनं नानुशोचितुमर्हसि ||२-२५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 25)

**Simple Meaning:**
The true self, your soul, is beyond what you can see or fully understand – it never changes or dies. Because of this, you shouldn't be sad when the body eventually passes away.

**Original Translation:**
It is said that the soul is invisible, inconceivable and immutable. Knowing this, you should not grieve for the body.

My grandmother passed away recently, and I'm really struggling with the grief. I keep thinking about all the moments I'll never share with her, but then I also wonder if part of her 'energy' or 'spirit' still exists in some form. Knowing that I can't be certain either way, how do I cope with this sadness and find peace?

अथ चैनं नित्यजातं नित्यं वा मन्यसे मृतम् |
तथापि त्वं महाबाहो नैवं शोचितुमर्हसि ||२-२६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 26)

**Simple Meaning:**
Whether you believe the soul is eternal and keeps being reborn, or that it simply dies with the body, Krishna is telling Arjuna there's no reason to be sad about death. Because either way, existence (or its form) continues in some way.

**Original Translation:**
If, however, you think that the soul [or the symptoms of life] will always be born and die forever, you still have no reason to lament, O mighty-armed.

My grandmother passed away recently, and I'm feeling really sad and anxious about my own mortality and the loss of loved ones.  I know life goes on, but it's hard to accept. How can I deal with this grief and find peace knowing that everyone I love will eventually face death, and I will too?

जातस्य हि ध्रुवो मृत्युर्ध्रुवं जन्म मृतस्य च |
तस्मादपरिहार्येऽर्थे न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि ||२-२७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 27)

**Simple Meaning:**
Birth and death are natural parts of life – they always happen. Since you can't escape them, don't get upset about the inevitable changes and focus on doing what you're meant to do.

**Original Translation:**
One who has taken his birth is sure to die, and after death one is sure to take birth again. Therefore, in the unavoidable discharge of your duty, you should not lament.

My grandmother recently passed away, and I'm feeling incredibly sad and lost. It feels like a huge part of my life is gone. Everyone keeps saying she lived a long life, but it still hurts so much. How can I cope with this grief knowing that nothing truly lasts forever?

अव्यक्तादीनि भूतानि व्यक्तमध्यानि भारत |
अव्यक्तनिधनान्येव तत्र का परिदेवना ||२-२८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 28)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything in the world has a beginning, a middle, and an end. Things aren't truly *created* or *destroyed*, they just change form. So worrying about loss or death is unnecessary because everything is a part of this constant cycle of change.

**Original Translation:**
All created beings are unmanifest in their beginning, manifest in their interim state, and unmanifest again when annihilated. So what need is there for lamentation?

My grandmother recently passed away, and everyone is talking about her 'spirit' or 'soul' living on. I hear different beliefs – some say she's in heaven, others talk about reincarnation, and some just say she's gone. It's all really confusing and overwhelming, and I'm not sure what to make of it. How can I make sense of this when people have such different ideas about something I can't even see or prove?

आश्चर्यवत्पश्यति कश्चिदेन-
माश्चर्यवद्वदति तथैव चान्यः |
आश्चर्यवच्चैनमन्यः शृणोति
श्रुत्वाप्येनं वेद न चैव कश्चित् ||२-२९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 29)

**Simple Meaning:**
The soul is beyond full comprehension. People react to it in different ways – some are awestruck by the idea, some talk about it with wonder, and others simply don't get it, even after hearing explanations. It’s a concept that’s hard to grasp fully.

**Original Translation:**
Some look on the soul as amazing, some describe him as amazing, and some hear of him as amazing, while others, even after hearing about him, cannot understand him at all.

I've been working really hard towards a promotion at work, but I keep getting passed over. I'm starting to feel really discouraged and like maybe I'm not good enough. I'm thinking of just giving up trying. What should I do?

क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते |
क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप ||२-३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 3)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, don't give in to feeling helpless or weak. It doesn't suit someone like you. Let go of this small-minded sadness and get up and do what you need to do.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Pṛthā, do not yield to this degrading impotence. It does not become you. Give up such petty weakness of heart and arise, O chastiser of the enemy.

My grandmother passed away recently, and I'm feeling completely heartbroken. It's hard to accept that she's gone, and I keep replaying memories in my head. Everyone says 'time heals all wounds,' but it doesn’t feel like it will. How can I cope with this immense loss and find peace knowing she's no longer physically here?

देही नित्यमवध्योऽयं देहे सर्वस्य भारत |
तस्मात्सर्वाणि भूतानि न त्वं शोचितुमर्हसि ||२-३०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 30)

**Simple Meaning:**
The soul, the true 'you' within the body, never dies. It just changes bodies like we change clothes. So, there's no reason to be sad or grieve over anyone's death, because the essence of that person always exists.

**Original Translation:**
O descendant of Bharata, he who dwells in the body can never be slain. Therefore you need not grieve for any living being.

I'm a police officer, and I've discovered widespread corruption within my department. Reporting it could ruin my career and put my family at risk, but ignoring it feels deeply wrong. Should I risk everything to do what's right, even if it means personal sacrifice, or should I prioritize my family's safety and look the other way?

स्वधर्ममपि चावेक्ष्य न विकम्पितुमर्हसि |
धर्म्याद्धि युद्धाच्छ्रेयोऽन्यत्क्षत्रियस्य न विद्यते ||२-३१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 31)

**Simple Meaning:**
As a warrior, your most important job is to fight for what's right and uphold justice. Avoiding this duty, even when it's hard, isn't honorable or beneficial for you. It's your core purpose.

**Original Translation:**
Considering your specific duty as a kṣatriya, you should know that there is no better engagement for you than fighting on religious principles; and so there is no need for hesitation.

I'm a police officer and constantly facing dangerous situations while trying to protect my community. It's emotionally draining and sometimes feels pointless with all the negativity. Even though I signed up for this job, I'm wondering how to find meaning and purpose in these stressful, life-threatening experiences instead of just seeing them as horrific events.

यदृच्छया चोपपन्नं स्वर्गद्वारमपावृतम् |
सुखिनः क्षत्रियाः पार्थ लभन्ते युद्धमीदृशम् ||२-३२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 32)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, you should know that warriors who get a chance to fight in a righteous war are truly fortunate. This kind of battle isn't a burden, but an opportunity to earn spiritual merit and move towards a better afterlife (heaven). It's a doorway to growth, even if it seems difficult.

**Original Translation:**
O Pārtha, happy are the kṣatriyas to whom such fighting opportunities come unsought, opening for them the doors of the heavenly planets.

I'm a doctor, and a patient asked me to perform a risky surgery that could save their life, but it goes against my ethical guidelines. I'm afraid of the potential complications and legal issues. If I refuse to do it, knowing it could mean the patient's death, am I doing the right thing, or am I failing in my duty as a doctor?

अथ चेत्त्वमिमं धर्म्यं संग्रामं न करिष्यसि |
ततः स्वधर्मं कीर्तिं च हित्वा पापमवाप्स्यसि ||२-३३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 33)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you avoid doing what you're meant to do – your duty, especially when it's the right thing – you'll face negative consequences and lose respect. Ignoring your responsibilities isn't a path to peace or honor.

**Original Translation:**
If, however, you do not perform your religious duty of fighting, then you will certainly incur sins for neglecting your duties and thus lose your reputation as a fighter.

I'm a doctor and I made a tough call in a surgery that saved the patient's life, but a family member is publicly accusing me of negligence online, saying I was reckless. It's really damaging my reputation, and I'm stressed about losing patients. Should I focus on defending myself and my career, or prioritize the fact that the patient is alive and recovering?

अकीर्तिं चापि भूतानि कथयिष्यन्ति तेऽव्ययाम् |
सम्भावितस्य चाकीर्तिर्मरणादतिरिच्यते ||२-३४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 34)

**Simple Meaning:**
People will talk, both good and bad, no matter what you do. But for someone who values their reputation, being shamed or disgraced is far more painful than physical death. Worrying about what others say shouldn't stop you from doing what's right.

**Original Translation:**
People will always speak of your infamy, and for a respectable person, dishonor is worse than death.

I'm stepping down from a high-profile project at work because it's not aligning with my values anymore, and I feel it's the right decision for my mental health. But, I'm worried my colleagues will think I'm quitting because I'm afraid of a challenge or can't handle the pressure. How do I deal with that potential misjudgment and protect my reputation?

भयाद्रणादुपरतं मंस्यन्ते त्वां महारथाः |
येषां च त्वं बहुमतो भूत्वा यास्यसि लाघवम् ||२-३५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 35)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who respect your reputation will misunderstand your reasons for leaving the fight. They'll assume you acted out of fear or weakness, and it will damage your honor.

**Original Translation:**
The great generals who have highly esteemed your name and fame will think that you have left the battlefield out of fear only, and thus they will consider you insignificant.

I've been working really hard on this new project at work, but some colleagues are constantly making snide remarks about my ideas and saying they're unrealistic. It's really getting me down and making me doubt myself. How do I deal with people trying to tear down my confidence and work when I know I'm doing my best?

अवाच्यवादांश्च बहून्वदिष्यन्ति तवाहिताः |
निन्दन्तस्तव सामर्थ्यं ततो दुःखतरं नु किम् ||२-३६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 36)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who oppose you will say negative things about you and try to diminish your strengths. But what could cause you more suffering than that kind of criticism?

**Original Translation:**
Your enemies will describe you in many unkind words and scorn your ability. What could be more painful for you?

I'm starting a really tough new business venture, and honestly, I'm terrified of failing. I've put everything on the line. There's a chance it could completely flop and I'll lose a lot, but there's also a chance it could be hugely successful. Should I just give up now to avoid the risk of failure, or push forward even though it’s scary? What’s the right mindset to have?

हतो वा प्राप्स्यसि स्वर्गं जित्वा वा भोक्ष्यसे महीम् |
तस्मादुत्तिष्ठ कौन्तेय युद्धाय कृतनिश्चयः ||२-३७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 37)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, either you'll die a noble death in battle and go to heaven, or you'll win and rule the earth. So, stop worrying and commit to fighting with full resolve.

**Original Translation:**
O son of Kuntī, either you will be killed on the battlefield and attain the heavenly planets, or you will conquer and enjoy the earthly kingdom. Therefore, get up with determination and fight.

I'm starting my own business, and I'm terrified of failure. I'm constantly stressed about whether it will succeed or not, and it's making it hard to even begin. I'm putting so much pressure on myself to make it 'work'. How can I deal with this anxiety and just *start* building my business without being paralyzed by the fear of losing money or not achieving my goals?

सुखदुःखे समे कृत्वा लाभालाभौ जयाजयौ |
ततो युद्धाय युज्यस्व नैवं पापमवाप्स्यसि ||२-३८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 38)

**Simple Meaning:**
Focus on doing your duty without worrying about the results – whether things go well or badly, you win or lose. If you act with this detached mindset, you won't create negative consequences for yourself.

**Original Translation:**
Do thou fight for the sake of fighting, without considering happiness or distress, loss or gain, victory or defeat – and by so doing you shall never incur sin.

I'm really stressed about my career. I'm putting in a lot of effort, but I'm constantly anxious about whether I'll get promoted, if my projects will succeed, and what everyone thinks of my work. It's making me miserable and I'm starting to doubt myself. How can I focus on doing my best work without being so obsessed with the outcome?

एषा तेऽभिहिता साङ्ख्ये बुद्धिर्योगे त्विमां शृणु |
बुद्ध्या युक्तो यया पार्थ कर्मबन्धं प्रहास्यसि ||२-३९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 39)

**Simple Meaning:**
I've explained the philosophical understanding of things so far. Now, I'll tell you how to actually *do* things without getting caught up in worrying about the results. If you act with this understanding, you can break free from the cycle of being attached to what happens after your actions.

**Original Translation:**
Thus far I have described this knowledge to you through analytical study. Now listen as I explain it in terms of working without fruitive results. O son of Pṛthā, when you act in such knowledge you can free yourself from the bondage of works.

My boss has always been a mentor to me, really helped me grow in my career. Now, our company is restructuring, and I'm being asked to lead a project that directly competes with a new initiative my boss is championing. It feels incredibly disloyal and wrong to potentially undermine his work, even though it's what's best for the company. How do I navigate this situation when it feels like I have to 'fight' someone I deeply respect and owe so much to?

अर्जुन उवाच |
कथं भीष्ममहं सङ्ख्ये द्रोणं च मधुसूदन |
इषुभिः प्रतियोत्स्यामि पूजार्हावरिसूदन ||२-४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 4)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna is troubled by the thought of fighting against his respected teachers, Bhishma and Drona, whom he holds in high regard. He's struggling with the idea of raising his weapon against those he deeply admires and feels obligated to honor.

**Original Translation:**
Arjuna said: O killer of enemies, O killer of Madhu, how can I counterattack with arrows in battle men like Bhīṣma and Droṇa, who are worthy of my worship?

I've been wanting to start a business for years, but I keep getting stuck in the planning phase and afraid of failing. I feel overwhelmed and haven’t really *started* anything. Should I just give up, or is there value in taking small steps even if I'm not sure I'll succeed?

नेहाभिक्रमनाशोऽस्ति प्रत्यवायो न विद्यते |
स्वल्पमप्यस्य धर्मस्य त्रायते महतो भयात् ||२-४०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 40)

**Simple Meaning:**
Trying to do what’s right, even a little bit, never goes to waste. It shields you from huge regrets and anxieties in life, even if you stumble along the way. It’s about the effort, not just the perfect result.

**Original Translation:**
In this endeavor there is no loss or diminution, and a little advancement on this path can protect one from the most dangerous type of fear.

I'm constantly starting new projects – a blog, learning to code, a fitness routine – but I never finish anything because I get distracted by the next shiny idea. How do I find the discipline to stick to one thing and see it through?

व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिरेकेह कुरुनन्दन |
बहुशाखा ह्यनन्ताश्च बुद्धयोऽव्यवसायिनाम् ||२-४१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 41)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you want to succeed, focus on one goal with full dedication. People who lack focus get scattered with lots of different ideas and don't achieve much.

**Original Translation:**
Those who are on this path are resolute in purpose, and their aim is one. O beloved child of the Kurus, the intelligence of those who are irresolute is many-branched.

My friend keeps chasing promotions at work and is constantly stressed about earning more money and achieving a higher status, believing that's the key to being happy. He dismisses my advice about finding inner peace and says 'you need to succeed in the real world'. How can I explain to him that maybe there's more to life than just external achievements and focusing on results?

यामिमां पुष्पितां वाचं प्रवदन्त्यविपश्चितः |
वेदवादरताः पार्थ नान्यदस्तीति वादिनः ||२-४२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 42)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who don't deeply understand life often get caught up in the promises of rituals and good deeds described in the Vedas – hoping for a better rebirth, more power, or a comfortable life. They think these are the only ways to happiness, and don't look deeper.

**Original Translation:**
Men of small knowledge are very much attached to the flowery words of the Vedas, which recommend various fruitive activities for elevation to heavenly planets, resultant good birth, power, and so forth.

I've worked really hard my whole life to build a comfortable life – a nice house, a good car, a successful career. But lately, I'm feeling empty and unfulfilled. I keep chasing the next promotion, the next material possession, but it doesn't seem to make me any happier. Is it possible I'm focusing on the wrong things, and if so, how do I find something more meaningful?

कामात्मानः स्वर्गपरा जन्मकर्मफलप्रदाम् |
क्रियाविशेषबहुलां भोगैश्वर्यगतिं प्रति ||२-४३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 43)

**Simple Meaning:**
People often chase pleasure and luxury, believing that these things are the ultimate goal in life and that there's nothing beyond them. They focus solely on enjoying the world and the rewards it offers, without considering deeper meaning or purpose.

**Original Translation:**
Being desirous of sense gratification and opulent life, they say that there is nothing more than this.

I'm a successful entrepreneur, and I've built a comfortable life for myself. But lately, I feel empty despite having everything I thought I wanted. I'm constantly stressed about maintaining my lifestyle and acquiring more. I feel like I'm running on a treadmill and can't seem to find lasting happiness or purpose. How can I shift my focus and find something more fulfilling?

भोगैश्वर्यप्रसक्तानां तयापहृतचेतसाम् |
व्यवसायात्मिका बुद्धिः समाधौ न विधीयते ||२-४४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 44)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you're overly focused on enjoying pleasures and accumulating wealth, it clouds your judgment and makes it really hard to focus on spiritual growth or connecting with something bigger than yourself. It's difficult to be truly dedicated to anything meaningful when your mind is constantly chasing temporary satisfaction.

**Original Translation:**
In the minds of those who are too attached to sense enjoyment and material opulence, and who are bewildered by such things, the resolute determination for devotional service to the Supreme Lord does not take place.

I'm constantly stressed about my career – I want to succeed and earn a lot of money, but I'm also terrified of failing and losing everything. I feel like I'm always chasing the next promotion or worrying about job security. How can I find a sense of calm and focus amidst all this ambition and fear, and not let external success or failure dictate my happiness?

त्रैगुण्यविषया वेदा निस्त्रैगुण्यो भवार्जुन |
निर्द्वन्द्वो नित्यसत्त्वस्थो निर्योगक्षेम आत्मवान् ||२-४५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 45)

**Simple Meaning:**
The scriptures often talk about good, bad, and neutral experiences, but true peace comes from rising above these feelings. Don't get caught up in wanting things to go your way or fearing things going wrong. Focus on your inner self, and you'll find stability and freedom from worry.

**Original Translation:**
The Vedas deal mainly with the subject of the three modes of material nature. O Arjuna, become transcendental to these three modes. Be free from all dualities and from all anxieties for gain and safety, and be established in the self.

I'm feeling overwhelmed trying to learn everything about spirituality – different books, practices, philosophies... it's a lot! Is it really necessary to become an expert in all of it, or is there a simpler way to find what I’m looking for and make progress?

यावानर्थ उदपाने सर्वतः सम्प्लुतोदके |
तावान्सर्वेषु वेदेषु ब्राह्मणस्य विजानतः ||२-४६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 46)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like a small well and a big lake both give you water, all the teachings of the Vedas can be understood and fulfilled by knowing the main goal – self-realization. It's not about studying everything, but understanding the core message.

**Original Translation:**
All purposes served by a small well can at once be served by a great reservoir of water. Similarly, all the purposes of the Vedas can be served to one who knows the purpose behind them.

I've been working really hard on launching my own business, but it's been tough. I'm constantly stressed about whether it will succeed or fail, and I'm starting to feel paralyzed by the pressure. Should I just give up now before I get even more disappointed? How can I keep going even when I don’t see immediate results?

कर्मण्येवाधिकारस्ते मा फलेषु कदाचन |
मा कर्मफलहेतुर्भूर्मा ते सङ्गोऽस्त्वकर्मणि ||२-४७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 47)

**Simple Meaning:**
Focus on doing your best work without worrying about the outcome. Your job is to act, not to control what happens next. Don't let the fear of failure or the desire for rewards stop you from doing what you're meant to do, and don't avoid action altogether.

**Original Translation:**
You have a right to perform your prescribed duty, but you are not entitled to the fruits of action. Never consider yourself the cause of the results of your activities, and never be attached to not doing your duty.

I've been working really hard on a big project at work, but I'm constantly stressed about whether it will succeed or fail. It's affecting my sleep and my relationships. I feel like my happiness depends on the outcome. How can I work towards my goals without letting the possibility of failure completely derail me, and stay calm regardless of the result?

योगस्थः कुरु कर्माणि सङ्गं त्यक्त्वा धनञ्जय |
सिद्ध्यसिद्ध्योः समो भूत्वा समत्वं योग उच्यते ||२-४८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 48)

**Simple Meaning:**
Do your work with focus and dedication, but don't get too hung up on the results. Don't be overjoyed if things go well, and don't be crushed if they don't. Staying balanced and even-minded in all outcomes – that’s what true inner peace and skill comes from.

**Original Translation:**
Perform your duty equipoised, O Arjuna, abandoning all attachment to success or failure. Such equanimity is called yoga.

I'm constantly stressed about my career and achieving success – promotions, salary, recognition. It feels like I can't enjoy my work because I'm always thinking about what I'll *get* from it. How can I shift my focus and find peace even if things don't go exactly as planned?

दूरेण ह्यवरं कर्म बुद्धियोगाद्धनञ्जय |
बुद्धौ शरणमन्विच्छ कृपणाः फलहेतवः ||२-४९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 49)

**Simple Meaning:**
Focus on doing your duty with devotion and surrender to a higher power, instead of getting hung up on the rewards. People who only care about getting results are driven by selfishness and find it hard to be truly happy.

**Original Translation:**
O Dhanañjaya, keep all abominable activities far distant by devotional service, and in that consciousness surrender unto the Lord. Those who want to enjoy the fruits of their work are misers.

I'm starting my own business and a really successful competitor is offering me a partnership, but it involves using some of their unethical business practices that I'm not comfortable with. They're saying it's the only way to grow quickly and dominate the market. Should I accept their offer, even if it means compromising my values and potentially harming others?

गुरूनहत्वा हि महानुभावान्
श्रेयो भोक्तुं भैक्ष्यमपीह लोके |
हत्वार्थकामांस्तु गुरूनिहैव
भुञ्जीय भोगान् रुधिरप्रदिग्धान् ||२-५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 5)

**Simple Meaning:**
It's better to live a simple life, even if it means hardship, than to gain success by harming or disrespecting your teachers or those wiser than you. Any benefits you get from harming them will always feel wrong and won't bring true happiness.

**Original Translation:**
It would be better to live in this world by begging than to live at the cost of the lives of great souls who are my teachers. Even though desiring worldly gain, they are superiors. If they are killed, everything we enjoy will be tainted with blood.

I'm a freelancer and constantly stressed about landing clients and getting good reviews. I'm either elated when I get a project or worried sick about the next one, and it's affecting my health and relationships.  How can I get to a point where I can just focus on doing my best work without being so emotionally attached to the results?

बुद्धियुक्तो जहातीह उभे सुकृतदुष्कृते |
तस्माद्योगाय युज्यस्व योगः कर्मसु कौशलम् ||२-५०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 50)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you focus on doing your work with dedication and without worrying about the results – good or bad – you free yourself from the cycle of getting stuck on outcomes.  The key is to focus on skillful action itself, and that's what yoga truly is: mastering your work and doing it with your whole heart.

**Original Translation:**
A man engaged in devotional service rids himself of both good and bad reactions even in this life. Therefore strive for yoga, which is the art of all work.

I'm a doctor and I constantly feel stressed about whether my patients will recover, even when I've done everything I can with my skills and knowledge. It's affecting my mental health. How can I keep doing my best work without getting overwhelmed by the outcome of each case?

कर्मजं बुद्धियुक्ता हि फलं त्यक्त्वा मनीषिणः |
जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः पदं गच्छन्त्यनामयम् ||२-५१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 51)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you focus on doing your duty with devotion, without worrying about the rewards or results, you can break free from the endless cycle of birth, death, and suffering. This leads you to a state of lasting peace and liberation.

**Original Translation:**
By thus engaging in devotional service to the Lord, great sages or devotees free themselves from the results of work in the material world. In this way they become free from the cycle of birth and death and attain the state beyond all miseries [by going back to Godhead].

I've been researching different career paths for months, constantly reading articles, taking online courses, and talking to people. It's overwhelming! Every path seems good, but I keep second-guessing myself, worried I'll make the wrong decision and waste time and money. How do I find peace of mind and stop getting caught in endless analysis paralysis, and actually *choose* something?

यदा ते मोहकलिलं बुद्धिर्व्यतितरिष्यति |
तदा गन्तासि निर्वेदं श्रोतव्यस्य श्रुतस्य च ||२-५२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 52)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you overcome confusion and attachment to outcomes – when your mind isn't clouded by desires or worries about what you've learned or what's yet to come – you'll reach a state of peaceful detachment. You won't be swayed by opinions or new information, and you’ll find clarity.

**Original Translation:**
When your intelligence has passed out of the dense forest of delusion, you shall become indifferent to all that has been heard and all that is to be heard.

I've been jumping from one self-help book to another, one meditation technique to the next, constantly seeking answers outside of myself. I feel overwhelmed and more confused than ever. How do I find peace and clarity when there's so much conflicting information and so many different paths to follow?

श्रुतिविप्रतिपन्ना ते यदा स्थास्यति निश्चला |
समाधावचला बुद्धिस्तदा योगमवाप्स्यसि ||२-५३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 53)

**Simple Meaning:**
True understanding comes when you stop getting distracted by complicated ideas or rituals and find a peaceful, focused state within yourself. It's about reaching a calm inner knowing, not just intellectual knowledge.

**Original Translation:**
When your mind is no longer disturbed by the flowery language of the Vedas, and when it remains fixed in the trance of self-realization, then you will have attained the divine consciousness.

My friend recently quit their high-paying job to 'find themselves' and now spends hours in quiet contemplation. They seem calm, but I'm worried about their future and if they're actually okay. How can I tell if they're genuinely experiencing inner peace and growth, or if they're just avoiding reality? What would a person who's truly found contentment *look* like?

अर्जुन उवाच |
स्थितप्रज्ञस्य का भाषा समाधिस्थस्य केशव |
स्थितधीः किं प्रभाषेत किमासीत व्रजेत किम् ||२-५४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 54)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna is asking Krishna to describe what a truly enlightened person is *like*. He wants to know how someone who's found inner peace acts and speaks – what are the visible signs of this deep state of meditation and wisdom?

**Original Translation:**
Arjuna said: O Kṛṣṇa, what are the symptoms of one whose consciousness is thus merged in transcendence? How does he speak, and what is his language? How does he sit, and how does he walk?

I've been working really hard to get a promotion at work, thinking it will finally make me happy. But even if I get it, I worry I'll just immediately start wanting the *next* thing. I feel like I'm constantly chasing something, never truly satisfied. How can I find lasting happiness instead of relying on achievements or possessions?

श्रीभगवानुवाच |
प्रजहाति यदा कामान्सर्वान्पार्थ मनोगतान् |
आत्मन्येवात्मना तुष्टः स्थितप्रज्ञस्तदोच्यते ||२-५५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 55)

**Simple Meaning:**
Krishna explains that when you stop chasing after things that make you happy temporarily – things you *think* you want – and instead find peace and satisfaction within yourself, you reach a state of true contentment and understanding. It’s about letting go of external desires and finding happiness in who you are.

**Original Translation:**
The Supreme Personality of Godhead said: O Pārtha, when a man gives up all varieties of desire for sense gratification, which arise from mental concoction, and when his mind, thus purified, finds satisfaction in the self alone, then he is said to be in pure transcendental consciousness.

I'm constantly stressed about work – if things are going well, I worry about losing my job, and if things are tough, I get completely overwhelmed.  It feels like a never-ending cycle. How can I find a way to be more even-keeled and stop letting external events control my emotions?

दुःखेष्वनुद्विग्नमनाः सुखेषु विगतस्पृहः |
वीतरागभयक्रोधः स्थितधीर्मुनिरुच्यते ||२-५६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 56)

**Simple Meaning:**
A truly wise person stays calm and balanced no matter what happens – they don't get too upset by problems or too excited by good times. They aren't controlled by their desires, fears, or anger, and have a peaceful, stable mind.

**Original Translation:**
One who is not disturbed in mind even amidst the threefold miseries or elated when there is happiness, and who is free from attachment, fear and anger, is called a sage of steady mind.

I just got passed over for a promotion at work that I really wanted, and I'm feeling incredibly frustrated and demotivated. I’ve worked so hard, and now I’m questioning everything. How can I deal with this disappointment and not let it completely ruin my outlook and affect my performance?

यः सर्वत्रानभिस्नेहस्तत्तत्प्राप्य शुभाशुभम् |
नाभिनन्दति न द्वेष्टि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ||२-५७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 57)

**Simple Meaning:**
True understanding comes from being emotionally detached from the ups and downs of life – success or failure, good luck or bad. It's about not getting too happy with the good things and not getting too upset about the bad. When you don't let things control your emotions, you find inner peace and clarity.

**Original Translation:**
In the material world, one who is unaffected by whatever good or evil he may obtain, neither praising it nor despising it, is firmly fixed in perfect knowledge.

I'm constantly checking my phone – social media, news, emails – and it's making me anxious and stressed. I feel like I can't focus on my work or even enjoy time with my family. How can I find a way to disconnect and be more present in the moment, like the verse describes with controlling the senses?

यदा संहरते चायं कूर्मोऽङ्गानीव सर्वशः |
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ||२-५८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 58)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse says that true peace of mind comes from being able to control your senses and not get distracted by external things. Just like a tortoise can pull all its limbs inside its shell, we need to be able to withdraw our attention from what we see, hear, and desire to find inner focus and stability.

**Original Translation:**
One who is able to withdraw his senses from sense objects, as the tortoise draws its limbs within the shell, is firmly fixed in perfect consciousness.

I've been trying to cut back on social media and junk food, but I keep getting strong cravings and the urge to check my phone. It feels like I'm constantly fighting myself. How do I actually overcome these desires instead of just suppressing them? Is there a way to find something that makes these bad habits feel less appealing?

विषया विनिवर्तन्ते निराहारस्य देहिनः |
रसवर्जं रसोऽप्यस्य परं दृष्ट्वा निवर्तते ||२-५९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 59)

**Simple Meaning:**
Even if you try to control your senses and stop enjoying things, the *desire* for those enjoyments doesn't disappear easily. True freedom comes when you find something even more fulfilling – a deeper, higher experience – that naturally makes you lose interest in lesser pleasures.

**Original Translation:**
Though the embodied soul may be restricted from sense enjoyment, the taste for sense objects remains. But, ceasing such engagements by experiencing a higher taste, he is fixed in consciousness.

My startup is failing, and I'm facing a tough choice: keep pouring money into it, hoping for a turnaround, or shut it down and cut my losses. Either way, I feel like I'm going to lose – my investment if I keep going, or my dream if I quit. It feels like there's no good option, and I don't even know if fighting for it is worthwhile anymore. What should I do?

न चैतद्विद्मः कतरन्नो गरीयो
यद्वा जयेम यदि वा नो जयेयुः |
यानेव हत्वा न जिजीविषामस्-
तेऽवस्थिताः प्रमुखे धार्तराष्ट्राः ||२-६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 6)

**Simple Meaning:**
The Kauravas are unsure if winning the war is even worth it. They feel that even if they defeat Dhritarashtra's sons, life won't have any meaning, and if they lose, the future is bleak. They are trapped in a difficult situation, facing a grim reality on the battlefield.

**Original Translation:**
Nor do we know which is better – conquering them or being conquered by them. If we killed the sons of Dhṛtarāṣṭra, we should not care to live. Yet they are now standing before us on the battlefield.

I'm trying to stick to my fitness goals and eat healthy, but I keep getting distracted by food ads on social media and the tempting snacks my coworkers bring in. I *want* to be disciplined, but it feels like my cravings and impulses are winning. How do I deal with this constant battle between what I want to do and what my senses are telling me to do?

यततो ह्यपि कौन्तेय पुरुषस्य विपश्चितः |
इन्द्रियाणि प्रमाथीनि हरन्ति प्रसभं मनः ||२-६०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 60)

**Simple Meaning:**
Even someone who *tries* really hard to control their senses can get overwhelmed by them. Our senses are powerful and constantly pulling us in different directions, making it tough to focus and stay in control of our thoughts.

**Original Translation:**
The senses are so strong and impetuous, O Arjuna, that they forcibly carry away the mind even of a man of discrimination who is endeavoring to control them.

I'm constantly scrolling through social media, checking work emails even on my days off, and getting pulled in a million different directions by notifications. It feels like I can’t focus on anything important, and I'm always stressed. How can I find some peace and regain control of my attention so I can actually achieve my goals and enjoy life?

तानि सर्वाणि संयम्य युक्त आसीत मत्परः |
वशे हि यस्येन्द्रियाणि तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ||२-६१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 61)

**Simple Meaning:**
To have a clear and focused mind, you need to control your senses – what you see, hear, taste, smell, and touch – and dedicate your thoughts to something bigger than yourself, like a higher purpose or a spiritual ideal. When you're not distracted by your impulses, you gain real wisdom and stability.

**Original Translation:**
One who restrains his senses, keeping them under full control, and fixes his consciousness upon Me, is known as a man of steady intelligence.

I've been endlessly scrolling through social media, looking at everyone's perfect vacations and possessions. Now I feel really unhappy with my own life and super irritated whenever I see someone else enjoying something I don't have. Why am I feeling this way, and how can I stop it?

ध्यायतो विषयान्पुंसः सङ्गस्तेषूपजायते |
सङ्गात्सञ्जायते कामः कामात्क्रोधोऽभिजायते ||२-६२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 62)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you keep thinking about things you desire – nice stuff, pleasant experiences – you start to really want them. That wanting can turn into strong cravings, and when you don't get what you crave, it leads to frustration and anger.

**Original Translation:**
While contemplating the objects of the senses, a person develops attachment for them, and from such attachment lust develops, and from lust anger arises.

I keep getting into arguments with my partner, and after each fight, I feel like I completely forget what we were even disagreeing about, or why it mattered so much. I just end up repeating the same hurtful things. It's like I'm stuck in a cycle of conflict and I can't seem to break it. How can I stop this from happening and get a better handle on my emotions before they ruin my relationship?

क्रोधाद्भवति सम्मोहः सम्मोहात्स्मृतिविभ्रमः |
स्मृतिभ्रंशाद् बुद्धिनाशो बुद्धिनाशात्प्रणश्यति ||२-६३|| (Chapter 2, Verse 63)

**Simple Meaning:**
Anger clouds your judgment, leading to forgetfulness and poor decisions. When you keep making bad choices because you're not thinking clearly, you end up repeating mistakes and getting stuck in negative patterns.

**Original Translation:**
From anger, complete delusion arises, and from delusion bewilderment of memory. When memory is bewildered, intelligence is lost, and when intelligence is lost one falls down again into the material pool.

I'm really struggling with social media. I get so caught up in comparing myself to others and constantly chasing likes and validation. It's making me anxious and unhappy. How can I find peace and contentment instead of being driven by these external things and the need for approval?

रागद्वेषविमुक्तैस्तु विषयानिन्द्रियैश्चरन् | (or वियुक्तैस्तु)
आत्मवश्यैर्विधेयात्मा प्रसादमधिगच्छति ||२-६४|| (Chapter 2, Verse 64)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you can let go of strong likes and dislikes, and gain control over your senses with discipline, you'll be open to receiving God's grace and blessings.

**Original Translation:**
But a person free from all attachment and aversion and able to control his senses through regulative principles of freedom can obtain the complete mercy of the Lord.

I'm constantly stressed about work, relationships, and finances. It feels like I'm always chasing something, and even when I get it, I'm not happy. How can I find lasting peace and stop feeling so overwhelmed by life's problems?

प्रसादे सर्वदुःखानां हानिरस्योपजायते |
प्रसन्नचेतसो ह्याशु बुद्धिः पर्यवतिष्ठते ||२-६५|| (Chapter 2, Verse 65)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you find peace and contentment in connecting with something bigger than yourself (like Krishna consciousness), the everyday pains and worries of life start to fade away. With a clear and peaceful mind, you'll be able to think and make better decisions.

**Original Translation:**
For one thus satisfied [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness], the threefold miseries of material existence exist no longer; in such satisfied consciousness, one’s intelligence is soon well established.

I'm constantly stressed and overwhelmed with work and social expectations. I keep chasing the next promotion or the 'perfect' life online, but I never feel satisfied. Even when I achieve something, the feeling doesn't last. I'm always anxious and feel like something is missing. How can I find lasting peace and contentment instead of just temporary highs?

नास्ति बुद्धिरयुक्तस्य न चायुक्तस्य भावना |
न चाभावयतः शान्तिरशान्तस्य कुतः सुखम् ||२-६६|| (Chapter 2, Verse 66)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you're not connected to something bigger than yourself – a higher purpose or a sense of spirituality – you won't have clarity or mental stability. Without that stability, true peace is impossible, and happiness simply won't last. It's like trying to build a house on shaky ground.

**Original Translation:**
One who is not connected with the Supreme [in Kṛṣṇa consciousness] can have neither transcendental intelligence nor a steady mind, without which there is no possibility of peace. And how can there be any happiness without peace?

I'm trying to stick to a new workout routine and eat healthier, but every time I see a tempting dessert or a friend invites me for pizza, I completely abandon my plan. It feels like I have no willpower! How do I stay focused on my health goals when these cravings and temptations keep pulling me back?

इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते |
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि ||२-६७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 67)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like a powerful wind can easily blow a boat off course, even a single uncontrolled sense – like a strong desire or attraction – can completely distract you and lead you away from clear thinking and your goals. It's easy to lose focus and get carried away by your impulses.

**Original Translation:**
As a strong wind sweeps away a boat on the water, even one of the roaming senses on which the mind focuses can carry away a man’s intelligence.

I'm constantly getting distracted by social media, work emails, and just wanting to buy things online. I feel like I can't focus on important tasks or enjoy the present moment. How can I gain control of my impulses and find some mental clarity?

तस्माद्यस्य महाबाहो निगृहीतानि सर्वशः |
इन्द्रियाणीन्द्रियार्थेभ्यस्तस्य प्रज्ञा प्रतिष्ठिता ||२-६८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 68)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you can control your desires and focus your mind instead of constantly chasing what you want, you'll have clear thinking and inner peace. It's about mastering yourself, not letting your senses control you.

**Original Translation:**
Therefore, O mighty-armed, one whose senses are restrained from their objects is certainly of steady intelligence.

I'm a doctor working long, unpredictable shifts. I feel completely drained and have no energy for personal growth or even just relaxing. Everyone tells me to 'wind down' after work, but that feels impossible when my mind is still racing. How can I find time and energy for self-reflection and improvement when my life feels completely upside down?

या निशा सर्वभूतानां तस्यां जागर्ति संयमी |
यस्यां जाग्रति भूतानि सा निशा पश्यतो मुनेः ||२-६९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 69)

**Simple Meaning:**
What everyone else considers 'normal' and restful (like sleep or daily routine), a disciplined person uses as a time for focused self-awareness. Conversely, while others are busy and active, the wise person finds peace and clarity through introspection.

**Original Translation:**
What is night for all beings is the time of awakening for the self-controlled; and the time of awakening for all beings is night for the introspective sage.

I'm at a major crossroads in my career. I have a stable job, but I'm deeply unhappy. I'm afraid to take a risk and pursue my passion because of financial worries and the fear of failure. I'm paralyzed by doubt and can't seem to make a decision. What's the right thing for me to do, and how do I overcome my anxiety and find clarity?

कार्पण्यदोषोपहतस्वभावः
पृच्छामि त्वां धर्मसम्मूढचेताः |
यच्छ्रेयः स्यान्निश्चितं ब्रूहि तन्मे
शिष्यस्तेऽहं शाधि मां त्वां प्रपन्नम् ||२-७|| (Chapter 2, Verse 7)

**Simple Meaning:**
I'm feeling really lost and confused about what I should do, and my own doubts and anxieties are making it worse. I'm asking you to clearly tell me the best path forward. I'm ready to learn from you as your student and fully trust in your guidance.

**Original Translation:**
Now I am confused about my duty and have lost all composure because of miserly weakness. In this condition I am asking You to tell me for certain what is best for me. Now I am Your disciple, and a soul surrendered unto You. Please instruct me.

I'm constantly chasing the next promotion, bigger house, better car… I feel like I'll *finally* be happy when I achieve these things, but as soon as I get one, I just want the next one. I'm never truly satisfied. How can I find peace when I'm always wanting more?

आपूर्यमाणमचलप्रतिष्ठं
समुद्रमापः प्रविशन्ति यद्वत् |
तद्वत्कामा यं प्रविशन्ति सर्वे
स शान्तिमाप्नोति न कामकामी ||२-७०|| (Chapter 2, Verse 70)

**Simple Meaning:**
Just like the ocean remains calm even as rivers constantly flow into it, a peaceful person isn't shaken by desires that come their way. Trying to *get* everything you want won't bring happiness; inner peace comes from accepting desires without being controlled by them.

**Original Translation:**
A person who is not disturbed by the incessant flow of desires – that enter like rivers into the ocean, which is ever being filled but is always still – can alone achieve peace, and not the man who strives to satisfy such desires.

I'm constantly stressed because I'm always chasing the next promotion, bigger house, or more stuff. I feel empty even when I get what I want because I'm already thinking about the next thing. How can I find lasting peace and happiness instead of this constant cycle of wanting?

विहाय कामान्यः सर्वान्पुमांश्चरति निःस्पृहः |
निर्ममो निरहङ्कारः स शान्तिमधिगच्छति ||२-७१|| (Chapter 2, Verse 71)

**Simple Meaning:**
True peace comes from letting go of wanting things, not being attached to possessions or outcomes, and realizing you don't 'own' anything. It's about finding contentment without needing external things to make you happy.

**Original Translation:**
A person who has given up all desires for sense gratification, who lives free from desires, who has given up all sense of proprietorship and is devoid of false ego – he alone can attain real peace.

My grandmother is very sick, and honestly, I'm terrified of losing her, and of death itself. I feel so anxious and overwhelmed thinking about it. How can I find inner peace and strength during this difficult time, so I don't fall apart with grief and fear, and can support her (and myself) with calmness?

एषा ब्राह्मी स्थितिः पार्थ नैनां प्राप्य विमुह्यति |
स्थित्वास्यामन्तकालेऽपि ब्रह्मनिर्वाणमृच्छति ||२-७२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 72)

**Simple Meaning:**
This is the state of deep spiritual connection – when you're grounded in truth and not confused by life's ups and downs. If you can maintain this peace, even at the time of death, you'll find liberation and a connection with the divine.

**Original Translation:**
That is the way of the spiritual and godly life, after attaining which a man is not bewildered. If one is thus situated even at the hour of death, one can enter into the kingdom of God.

I'm feeling completely lost and overwhelmed with life. I've tried everything – different careers, relationships, hobbies – but nothing seems to bring lasting happiness or purpose. It's like I'm searching for something I can't even define. Where do I even *begin* to find clarity and direction in my life, and what's the most effective way to understand my true self?

ॐ तत्सदिति श्रीमद्भगवद्गीतासूपनिषत्सु
ब्रह्मविद्यायां योगशास्त्रे श्रीकृष्णार्जुनसंवादे
साङ्ख्ययोगो नाम द्वितीयोऽध्यायः ||२|| (Chapter 2, Verse 73)

**Simple Meaning:**
This verse introduces the second chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, called the 'Yoga of Knowledge' or 'Samkhya Yoga'. It signifies the beginning of the core philosophical discussion between Krishna and Arjuna about understanding reality and the path to liberation. It's basically setting the stage for a deep dive into wisdom and self-realization.

**Original Translation:**
Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

I've worked my entire life to build a successful career, and I've finally reached the top. But after achieving everything I wanted, I still feel empty and unhappy. I'm questioning the meaning of it all. How can I find peace and contentment when external achievements don't seem to bring lasting happiness?

न हि प्रपश्यामि ममापनुद्याद्
यच्छोकमुच्छोषणमिन्द्रियाणाम् |
अवाप्य भूमावसपत्नमृद्धं
राज्यं सुराणामपि चाधिपत्यम् ||२-८|| (Chapter 2, Verse 8)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna feels overwhelmed with sorrow and doesn't see how winning the kingdom or achieving any worldly success can actually make him feel better. He believes that no amount of power or achievement can alleviate his grief right now.

**Original Translation:**
I can find no means to drive away this grief which is drying up my senses. I will not be able to dispel it even if I win a prosperous, unrivaled kingdom on earth with sovereignty like the demigods in heaven.

I've been working towards a big promotion at my company for years, but now that it's within reach, I'm realizing it would mean having to make some tough decisions that go against my personal values. I'm really conflicted and feel like I'm at a standstill, unable to decide whether to pursue the promotion or walk away. How do I deal with this internal conflict when my desired goal now clashes with what I believe is right?

सञ्जय उवाच |
एवमुक्त्वा हृषीकेशं गुडाकेशः परन्तप |
न योत्स्य इति गोविन्दमुक्त्वा तूष्णीं बभूव ह ||२-९|| (Chapter 2, Verse 9)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna, after hearing Krishna’s teachings, declared to Krishna that he would not fight in the war. He said this and then stopped talking, overcome with emotion and uncertainty.

**Original Translation:**
Sañjaya said: Having spoken thus, Arjuna, chastiser of enemies, told Kṛṣṇa, “Govinda, I shall not fight,” and fell silent.

My boss keeps pushing me to take on increasingly stressful projects to 'look good' on paper and get a promotion, even though it’s burning me out and making me miserable. I value a healthy work-life balance and personal well-being. Should I keep sacrificing my happiness for career advancement, or is there a better way to approach this situation?

अर्जुन उवाच |
ज्यायसी चेत्कर्मणस्ते मता बुद्धिर्जनार्दन |
तत्किं कर्मणि घोरे मां नियोजयसि केशव ||३-१|| (Chapter 3, Verse 1)

**Simple Meaning:**
Arjuna is asking Krishna why he's being asked to fight a terrible war if Krishna believes wisdom and understanding are superior to just performing actions for the sake of results. He's questioning the logic – shouldn't they prioritize knowledge over a bloody battle?

**Original Translation:**
Arjuna said: O Janārdana, O Keśava, why do You want to engage me in this ghastly warfare, if You think that intelligence is better than fruitive work?

I'm feeling really lost and empty even though I have a good job and comfortable life. I just go through the motions each day. Everyone says I 'should' be happy, but I'm not. Could focusing on helping others and contributing to something bigger than myself actually make a difference in how I feel, and is that what this verse is trying to tell me?

सहयज्ञाः प्रजाः सृष्ट्वा पुरोवाच प्रजापतिः |
अनेन प्रसविष्यध्वमेष वोऽस्त्विष्टकामधुक् ||३-१०|| (Chapter 3, Verse 10)

**Simple Meaning:**
At the start of time, the Creator made people and gave them the practice of selfless service (yajña) – doing things for a greater purpose, not just for ourselves. He told them that by focusing on contribution and giving back, they’d find happiness and fulfillment in life, eventually leading to freedom from suffering.

**Original Translation:**
In the beginning of creation, the Lord of all creatures sent forth generations of men and demigods, along with sacrifices for Viṣṇu, and blessed them by saying, “Be thou happy by this yajña [sacrifice] because its performance will bestow upon you everything desirable for living happily and achieving liberation.”

I'm starting a new community garden, and I'm worried about getting buy-in from both the city council (who control the land) and the local residents. It feels like they have very different priorities. How can I create a situation where everyone feels respected and valued so we can all work together and make the garden thrive?

देवान्भावयतानेन ते देवा भावयन्तु वः |
परस्परं भावयन्तः श्रेयः परमवाप्स्यथ ||३-११|| (Chapter 3, Verse 11)

**Simple Meaning:**
When you show respect and offer good things to those you admire or see as powerful (like leaders, nature, or even principles you believe in), they will, in turn, support you. Mutual respect and helpfulness lead to success and well-being for everyone involved.

**Original Translation:**
The demigods, being pleased by sacrifices, will also please you, and thus, by cooperation between men and demigods, prosperity will reign for all.

I've been really successful at my job and making good money, but I feel strangely unfulfilled and like something's missing. I keep wanting more, but it doesn’t bring lasting happiness. I haven’t really done anything to help others or give back to the community. Could my unhappiness be related to just taking from life without offering anything in return?

इष्टान्भोगान्हि वो देवा दास्यन्ते यज्ञभाविताः |
तैर्दत्तानप्रदायैभ्यो यो भुङ्क्ते स्तेन एव सः ||३-१२|| (Chapter 3, Verse 12)

**Simple Meaning:**
The things we need in life come from a natural order, and offering gratitude (like through selfless work or contribution) keeps that flow going. Taking without giving back – being selfishly consuming without contributing – is like stealing from the system.

**Original Translation:**
In charge of the various necessities of life, the demigods, being satisfied by the performance of yajña [sacrifice], will supply all necessities to you. But he who enjoys such gifts without offering them to the demigods in return is certainly a thief.

I'm really stressed about always wanting more – more success, more things, more recognition. It feels like I'm constantly chasing something, and it never feels satisfying. I cook nice meals for myself as a reward, but it doesn't actually make me happier. Is there a way to change this cycle and find more peace, even while still enjoying life?

यज्ञशिष्टाशिनः सन्तो मुच्यन्ते सर्वकिल्बिषैः |
भुञ्जते ते त्वघं पापा ये पचन्त्यात्मकारणात् ||३-१३|| (Chapter 3, Verse 13)

**Simple Meaning:**
People who offer their food to God before eating are freed from negative consequences. Those who cook and eat only for themselves, without offering, are basically consuming only the negative energy created by selfish desires.

**Original Translation:**
The devotees of the Lord are released from all kinds of sins because they eat food which is offered first for sacrifice. Others, who prepare food for personal sense enjoyment, verily eat only sin.

I'm feeling really stuck and unmotivated. I'm just going through the motions at work and not seeing any positive results. It feels like my efforts don't matter and I'm not contributing anything. How can I find meaning in my work and feel like I'm actually making a difference, instead of just taking and not giving back?

अन्नाद्भवन्ति भूतानि पर्जन्यादन्नसम्भवः |
यज्ञाद्भवति पर्जन्यो यज्ञः कर्मसमुद्भवः ||३-१४|| (Chapter 3, Verse 14)

**Simple Meaning:**
Everything we eat comes from plants, and plants need rain to grow. Rain comes because of rituals and selfless work performed as duty. Essentially, life is sustained by a cycle of action and responsibility; what we give back to the world affects what we receive.

**Original Translation:**
All living bodies subsist on food grains, which are produced from rains. Rains are produced by performance of yajña [sacrifice], and yajña is born of prescribed duties.

I'm feeling really lost about what career path to choose. There are so many options and I'm worried about picking the 'wrong' one. Should I just chase the most lucrative option, or is there a way to find work that feels more meaningful and aligned with something bigger than myself? How do I know if what I'm doing actually *matters*?

कर्म ब्रह्मोद्भवं विद्धि ब्रह्माक्षरसमुद्भवम् |
तस्मात्सर्वगतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् ||३-१५|| (Chapter 3, Verse 15)

**Simple Meaning:**
Our duties and actions are rooted in spiritual knowledge, and that knowledge comes from a higher power. This higher power is present in everything, especially when we act with purpose and devotion – like through rituals or selfless service. Essentially, doing our best, guided by spiritual principles, connects us to the divine.

**Original Translation:**
Regulated activities are prescribed in the Vedas, and the Vedas are directly manifested from the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Consequently the all-pervading Transcendence is eternally situated in acts of sacrifice.

I'm working a really high-paying job, but it feels empty. My company's practices don't align with my values, and I'm just accumulating wealth for myself. Even though I have financial security, I feel unfulfilled and stressed. Should I prioritize finding work that's more purposeful, even if it means earning less money?

एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं नानुवर्तयतीह यः |
अघायुरिन्द्रियारामो मोघं पार्थ स जीवति ||३-१६|| (Chapter 3, Verse 16)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you don't contribute to the well-being of society and just focus on your own pleasure, you're not really living a meaningful life. The Vedas teach us to give back, and ignoring that path leads to emptiness and a sense of incompleteness.

**Original Translation:**
My dear Arjuna, one who does not follow in human life the cycle of sacrifice thus established by the Vedas certainly leads a life full of sin. Living only for the satisfaction of the senses, such a person lives in vain.

I'm constantly feeling pressured to achieve more – a better job, a bigger house, more possessions. My parents and friends keep telling me I should be aiming higher. But honestly, I'm pretty happy with my simple life and feel content as I am. Am I wrong to not want to constantly strive for 'more', and is it okay to just be satisfied with what I have?

यस्त्वात्मरतिरेव स्यादात्मतृप्तश्च मानवः |
आत्मन्येव च सन्तुष्टस्तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते ||३-१७|| (Chapter 3, Verse 17)

**Simple Meaning:**
If you find joy and fulfillment within yourself, and are content with who you are, then you don't need to chase external goals or feel obligated by societal expectations. True happiness comes from self-realization and inner peace.

**Original Translation:**
But for one who takes pleasure in the Self, whose human life is one of self-realization, and who is satisfied in the Self only, fully satiated – for him there is no duty.

I'm a freelance graphic designer and constantly stressing about finding new clients. Sometimes I feel like I *need* to take on any project that comes my way just to pay the bills, and other times I feel paralyzed by the fear of taking on work I don't truly enjoy. How can I find peace and clarity in my work, so I don't feel so dependent on external factors for my happiness and well-being?

नैव तस्य कृतेनार्थो नाकृतेनेह कश्चन |
न चास्य सर्वभूतेषु कश्चिदर्थव्यपाश्रयः ||३-१८|| (Chapter 3, Verse 18)

**Simple Meaning:**
Someone who truly understands their purpose doesn't get caught up in whether or not they *have* to do something, or *don't* have to do something.
